title: Independent politician

infobox.value: or **non-affiliated politician** is a [politician](/wiki/Politician "Politician") formally not affiliated with any [political party](/wiki/Political_party "Political party"). In accordance with the applicable [electoral law](/wiki/Electoral_law "Electoral law") which regulates [electoral process](/wiki/Electoral_process "Electoral process") in a [representative democracy](/wiki/Representative_democracy "Representative democracy"), such non-affiliated politicians are not [nominated](/wiki/Nomination "Nomination") by political parties, but most commonly by a qualified number of citizens, thus becoming *independent candidates*, or *civic candidates*. As in the case of party-candidates, an independent electoral candidacy can be individual (a single independent candidate for a [single-seat electoral unit](/wiki/Single-seat_electoral_unit "Single-seat electoral unit")), or collective ([electoral list](/wiki/Electoral_list "Electoral list") of independent candidates for a [multi-seat electoral unit](/wiki/Electoral_district#District_magnitude "Electoral district")).

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Individual not affiliated to any political party

An **independent politician** or **non-affiliated politician** is a [politician](/wiki/Politician "Politician") formally not affiliated with any [political party](/wiki/Political_party "Political party"). In accordance with the applicable [electoral law](/wiki/Electoral_law "Electoral law") which regulates [electoral process](/wiki/Electoral_process "Electoral process") in a [representative democracy](/wiki/Representative_democracy "Representative democracy"), such non-affiliated politicians are not [nominated](/wiki/Nomination "Nomination") by political parties, but most commonly by a qualified number of citizens, thus becoming *independent candidates*, or *civic candidates*. As in the case of party-candidates, an independent electoral candidacy can be individual (a single independent candidate for a [single-seat electoral unit](/wiki/Single-seat_electoral_unit "Single-seat electoral unit")), or collective ([electoral list](/wiki/Electoral_list "Electoral list") of independent candidates for a [multi-seat electoral unit](/wiki/Electoral_district#District_magnitude "Electoral district")).

Politicians may also become independents after losing or repudiating affiliation with a political party. Independents sometimes choose to form a party, alliance, or [technical group](/wiki/Technical_group "Technical group") with other independents, and may formally register that organization. Even where the word "independent" is used, such alliances can have much in common with a political party, especially if there is an organization which needs to approve the "independent" candidates.

There are various reasons why someone may stand for office as an independent. Some independent politicians disagree with the idea or concept of political parties; viewing them as politically [corrupt](/wiki/Political_corruption "Political corruption").[[1]](#cite_note-1) Others may have political views that do not align with the platforms of any political party and therefore they choose not to affiliate with them. Some independent politicians may be associated with a party, perhaps as former members of it or else have views that align with it, but choose not to stand in its name, or are unable to do so because the party in question has selected another candidate. Others may belong to or support a political party at the national level but believe they should not formally represent it (and thus be subject to its policies) at another level. In some cases, a politician may be a member of an unregistered party and therefore officially recognised as an independent.

## Africa

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Independent_politician&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Africa")]

### Algeria

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Independent_politician&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Algeria")]

The current president of Algeria, [Abdelmadjid Tebboune](/wiki/Abdelmadjid_Tebboune "Abdelmadjid Tebboune"), presented himself as an independent candidate during the [2019 presidential election](/wiki/2019_Algerian_presidential_election "2019 Algerian presidential election") campaign. However, he remained a member of the central committee of the [National Liberation Front (Algeria)](/wiki/National_Liberation_Front_(Algeria) "National Liberation Front (Algeria)") of which he has been a member since the 1970s.[[2]](#cite_note-2)[[3]](#cite_note-3) [Nadir Larbaoui](/wiki/Nadir_Larbaoui "Nadir Larbaoui"), who was the prime minister of Algeria until 2025, is also an independent.[[4]](#cite_note-4)

### Libya

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Independent_politician&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Libya")]

During the rule of [Muammar Gaddafi](/wiki/Muammar_Gaddafi "Muammar Gaddafi"), most political parties were banned; Gaddafi himself did not belong to any party. After his fall, political parties began to form but still do not have a major role in [Libyan](/wiki/Libya "Libya") society.[[5]](#cite_note-5)

### Namibia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Independent_politician&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Namibia")]

In the [2020 Namibian local and regional elections](/wiki/2020_Namibian_local_and_regional_elections "2020 Namibian local and regional elections"), independent politicians won four seats.

### Tunisia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Independent_politician&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Tunisia")]

The President of Tunisia [Kais Saied](/wiki/Kais_Saied "Kais Saied") was [elected in 2019](/wiki/2019_Tunisian_presidential_election "2019 Tunisian presidential election") and [re-elected in 2024](/wiki/2024_Tunisian_presidential_election "2024 Tunisian presidential election") by presenting himself as an independent. The heads of government [Hichem Mechichi](/wiki/Hichem_Mechichi "Hichem Mechichi"), [Najla Bouden](/wiki/Najla_Bouden "Najla Bouden"), [Ahmed Hachani](/wiki/Ahmed_Hachani "Ahmed Hachani") and [Kamel Madouri](/wiki/Kamel_Madouri "Kamel Madouri") also lack any official political affiliation.

## Americas

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Independent_politician&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Americas")]

### Brazil

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Independent_politician&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Brazil")]

Independent politicians are not allowed to run for office in Brazil. The Constitution of 1988, in Article 14, §3rd, item V, says that "Are conditions for eligibility: V - party affiliation".[[6]](#cite_note-6) However, the Proposal Amendment to the Constitution (PEC) no. 6/2015, authored by independent senator [José Reguffe](/wiki/Jos%C3%A9_Reguffe "José Reguffe"), would allow the independent candidacy of individuals who have the support of at least 1% of the electors able to vote in the region (city, state or country, depending on the election) in which the candidate is running.[[7]](#cite_note-7)[[8]](#cite_note-8) Currently, members of the legislature can leave their respective parties after being elected, as in the case of senator Reguffe, who left the [Democratic Labour Party](/wiki/Democratic_Labour_Party_(Brazil) "Democratic Labour Party (Brazil)") (PDT) in 2016.[[9]](#cite_note-9)
[Jair Bolsonaro](/wiki/Jair_Bolsonaro "Jair Bolsonaro") was independent between 2019 and 2021.

### Canada

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Independent_politician&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Canada")]

#### Federal politics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Independent_politician&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Federal politics")]

In Canadian federal politics, members of both the [House of Commons](/wiki/House_of_Commons_of_Canada "House of Commons of Canada") and the [Senate](/wiki/Senate_of_Canada "Senate of Canada") are permitted to hold office without being members of a political party. Candidates in federal elections who are not affiliated with a party have two options: independent or no affiliation. In the former case, they appear on the ballot with "Independent" following their name; in the second case, they appear with their name only. The two options are otherwise equivalent.

##### House of Commons

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Independent_politician&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: House of Commons")]

During the earliest Canadian Parliaments, a lack of coherent political identity among both the [Liberal](/wiki/Liberal_Party_of_Canada "Liberal Party of Canada") and [Conservative](/wiki/Conservative_Party_of_Canada_(1867%E2%80%931942) "Conservative Party of Canada (1867–1942)") parties is known to have led to [Members of Parliament](/wiki/Member_of_Parliament_(Canada) "Member of Parliament (Canada)") (MPs) occasionally demonstrating independence from their party by voting in line with the opposition.[[10]](#cite_note-GodboutHoyland2013-10) Commonly, the issues which caused these MPs to act independently were religious in nature.[[10]](#cite_note-GodboutHoyland2013-10) These tensions began to disperse over the course of the first ten Canadian parliaments as the major political parties began to form consistent identities and MPs began affiliating themselves with the parties they knew more closely shared their core values. This in turn increased cohesion between parties and MPs, and minimized the causes and motivations for MPs to act independently.[[10]](#cite_note-GodboutHoyland2013-10)

Many observers of the Canadian House of Commons in the 21st century have noted its incredibly high party discipline. Few MPs choose to vote against their party's official stance on any given piece of legislation.[[11]](#cite_note-Marland2020-11) Between 2011 and 2013—the first two years of the [41st Canadian Parliament](/wiki/41st_Canadian_Parliament "41st Canadian Parliament"), following the [2011 Canadian federal election](/wiki/2011_Canadian_federal_election "2011 Canadian federal election")—the elected members of the governing [Conservative Party](/wiki/Conservative_Party_of_Canada "Conservative Party of Canada") voted as a unified group on 76% of all votes, while members of the Liberal Party did so on 90% of all votes, and members of the [New Democratic Party](/wiki/New_Democratic_Party "New Democratic Party") (NDP) did so on 100% of votes.[[11]](#cite_note-Marland2020-11) This unity further increased in subsequent years, as in the [42nd Parliament](/wiki/42nd_Canadian_Parliament "42nd Canadian Parliament"), following the [2015 election](/wiki/2015_Canadian_federal_election "2015 Canadian federal election"), the governing Liberal MPs voted identically on 99.6% of all votes, Conservative MPs on 99.5% of votes, and NDP MPs on 99.8% of votes.[[11]](#cite_note-Marland2020-11) (Conservatives, Liberals, and NDP were the only three parties with enough MPs to qualify for [official party status](/wiki/Official_party_status "Official party status") in the 41st and 42nd parliaments.) Thanks to this strong party discipline, it is uncommon to see politicians who are otherwise affiliated with any of the main political parties act independently of their party.

Though it is acceptable and accepted for politicians to serve as independent MPs, those who attempt to run as such often struggle to be elected without access to the resources of the major parties. As a result, there are seldom more than one or two independent MPs within modern Canadian Parliaments, with many who do sit as such being initially elected as a part of a major party before either leaving voluntarily or being removed.[[12]](#cite_note-12) In the first year of the [44th Canadian Parliament](/wiki/44th_Canadian_Parliament "44th Canadian Parliament"), the House of Commons featured one sitting independent member: [Kevin Vuong](/wiki/Kevin_Vuong "Kevin Vuong"), from the [Ontario](/wiki/Ontario "Ontario") [electoral district](/wiki/Electoral_district_(Canada) "Electoral district (Canada)"), or riding, of [Spadina—Fort York](/wiki/Spadina%E2%80%94Fort_York_(federal_electoral_district) "Spadina—Fort York (federal electoral district)").[[13]](#cite_note-13) Vuong had originally campaigned as a member of the Liberal Party during the [2021 federal election](/wiki/2021_Canadian_federal_election "2021 Canadian federal election") but was ejected from the party two days prior to the end of the vote due to controversy surrounding past allegations of sexual assault.[[14]](#cite_note-cbc20210922-14) Despite his removal from the Liberal Party, Vuong won the election for his riding and chose to take his seat as an independent, though this decision was met with controversy because many voters had not known that the Liberals had expelled him before casting their votes.[[14]](#cite_note-cbc20210922-14) In 2022, [Alain Rayes](/wiki/Alain_Rayes "Alain Rayes"), MP for the [Quebec](/wiki/Quebec "Quebec") riding of [Richmond—Arthabaska](/wiki/Richmond%E2%80%94Arthabaska "Richmond—Arthabaska"), resigned from the Conservative caucus to sit as an independent,[[15]](#cite_note-15) becoming the second independent MP of the 44th Parliament.

Independent politicians have on occasion held considerable sway in the [House of Commons of Canada](/wiki/House_of_Commons_of_Canada "House of Commons of Canada") in recent years, as Canada has been governed by successive [minority governments](/wiki/Minority_government "Minority government") (five of the seven that have been formed since the [2004 federal election](/wiki/2004_Canadian_federal_election "2004 Canadian federal election")) with independent MPs sometimes sharing in the [balance of power](/wiki/Balance_of_power_(parliament) "Balance of power (parliament)").

* In 2004, [Chuck Cadman](/wiki/Chuck_Cadman "Chuck Cadman") was elected to the House as an independent MP representing the [British Columbia](/wiki/British_Columbia "British Columbia") riding of [Surrey North](/wiki/Surrey_North_(federal_electoral_district) "Surrey North (federal electoral district)"). Cadman was first elected to represent the riding as a [Reform](/wiki/Reform_Party_of_Canada "Reform Party of Canada") member in the [1997 federal election](/wiki/1997_Canadian_federal_election "1997 Canadian federal election") and re-elected as a member of the [Canadian Alliance](/wiki/Canadian_Alliance "Canadian Alliance"), Reform's successor party, in the [2000 federal election](/wiki/2000_Canadian_federal_election "2000 Canadian federal election"). He sought the nomination for the Conservative Party (re-created in 2003 when the Alliance and [Progressive Conservatives](/wiki/Progressive_Conservative_Party_of_Canada "Progressive Conservative Party of Canada") merged) for the 2004 election but was unsuccessful. Having retained his seat with 43% of the vote in 2004, he died in office in 2005.
* Independent [André Arthur](/wiki/Andr%C3%A9_Arthur "André Arthur") was elected in the Quebec riding of [Portneuf—Jacques-Cartier](/wiki/Portneuf%E2%80%94Jacques-Cartier "Portneuf—Jacques-Cartier") in the [2006 federal election](/wiki/2006_Canadian_federal_election "2006 Canadian federal election") with 39% of the vote. He was the only independent to win a seat in that election; he was re-elected in the [2008 federal election](/wiki/2008_Canadian_federal_election "2008 Canadian federal election") with 33% of the vote. Arthur lost his seat in 2011.
* [Bill Casey](/wiki/Bill_Casey "Bill Casey"), the MP for the [Nova Scotia](/wiki/Nova_Scotia "Nova Scotia") riding of Cumberland—Colchester—Musquoduboit Valley, was expelled from the Conservative Party for voting against the [2007 budget](/wiki/2007_Canadian_federal_budget "2007 Canadian federal budget"). He also served as an independent, then ran as such in 2008 and retained his seat with 69% of the vote. Casey resigned from the Commons in 2009 but made a comeback in the same riding, now named [Cumberland—Colchester](/wiki/Cumberland%E2%80%94Colchester "Cumberland—Colchester"), when he was elected as a Liberal in 2015. He sat in the House for the 42nd Parliament and did not seek re-election in [2019](/wiki/2019_Canadian_federal_election "2019 Canadian federal election").
* In 2019, MP [Jody Wilson-Raybould](/wiki/Jody_Wilson-Raybould "Jody Wilson-Raybould") ran as an independent candidate in the riding of [Vancouver Granville](/wiki/Vancouver_Granville "Vancouver Granville") after being expelled from cabinet and the Liberal Party over the [SNC-Lavalin affair](/wiki/SNC-Lavalin_affair "SNC-Lavalin affair"). She was returned to Parliament with 32% of the vote. After sitting as an independent for the [43rd Parliament](/wiki/43rd_Canadian_Parliament "43rd Canadian Parliament"), Wilson-Raybould did not seek re-election in 2021.

##### Senate

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Independent_politician&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Senate")]

While traditionally framed as an "independent body of sober second thought", appointments to the Senate of Canada prior to 2016 were commonly seen as highly partisan, with the majority of Canadian senators identifying themselves as members of either the Liberal or Conservative parties and serving within their party's caucus.[[16]](#cite_note-Thomas2019-16) As these have been the only two parties to ever form government in Canada, only the Liberal and Conservative parties had been able to appoint new senators. Because Canadian senators are appointed by the [Governor General of Canada](/wiki/Governor_General_of_Canada "Governor General of Canada") on the advice of the [Prime Minister](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_Canada "Prime Minister of Canada") rather than being elected, senators were often accused of being appointed as a "reward" for service to the party in power,[[17]](#cite_note-Bridgman2020-17) and once appointed, of simply repeating the points and positions of their counterparts in the House of Commons rather than acting as a means of truly independent policy review.[[16]](#cite_note-Thomas2019-16)

In 2014, as a response to growing public disapproval of the Senate and the perceived problems brought about by senator partisanship, Liberal Party leader [Justin Trudeau](/wiki/Justin_Trudeau "Justin Trudeau") made the decision to expel all Liberal senators from the Liberal Party caucus. Trudeau would go on to call for an overall elimination of partisanship in the Senate and pledged to end the practice of partisan appointments for senators and transition to a new system of merit-based appointments if elected prime minister.[[18]](#cite_note-Macfarlane2021-18)

Following the election of a Liberal [majority government](/wiki/Majority_government "Majority government") in 2015, the [Independent Advisory Board for Senate Appointments](/wiki/Independent_Advisory_Board_for_Senate_Appointments "Independent Advisory Board for Senate Appointments") was established with the goal of filling Senate vacancies through a selection process based on political knowledge, merit, and perceived ability to act independently of partisan affiliation.[[18]](#cite_note-Macfarlane2021-18) This push to remove partisan ties from the Senate resulted in the creation of the [Independent Senators Group](/wiki/Independent_Senators_Group "Independent Senators Group"), a coalition of both newly appointed independent senators and formally partisan senators who had relinquished their formal party ties, alongside the also independent [Canadian Senators Group](/wiki/Canadian_Senators_Group "Canadian Senators Group") and [Progressive Senate Group](/wiki/Progressive_Senate_Group "Progressive Senate Group").[[17]](#cite_note-Bridgman2020-17)

By 2018, the majority of Canadian Senators were officially independent,[[17]](#cite_note-Bridgman2020-17) though some Liberal senators continued to remain affiliated with the political party despite no longer being permitted within the party caucus.[[16]](#cite_note-Thomas2019-16) Additionally, the Conservative Party elected not to remove its senators from the party caucus, and many Conservative Party senators kept their official partisan affiliations in public.[[16]](#cite_note-Thomas2019-16) During the 2019 federal election campaign, in response to reporters' questions, Conservative Party leader [Andrew Scheer](/wiki/Andrew_Scheer "Andrew Scheer") said that if his party were elected to form government and he became prime minister, he would reinstate the practice of partisan appointments to the Senate.[[19]](#cite_note-19)

The efforts to increase senatorial independence have led some to argue the Senate has developed an increase of importance and power in the legislative process. As of 2021, it was found that Canadian senators were facing increasing pressure from [lobbying](/wiki/Lobbying "Lobbying") groups on a variety of issues, suggesting the more independent Senate has a greater perceived influence over legislative issues.[[17]](#cite_note-Bridgman2020-17) Additionally, following the appointment of senators through the Independent Advisory Board for Senate Appointments, there has been a noted increase in the number of amendments the Senate has proposed for legislation from the House of Commons.[[16]](#cite_note-Thomas2019-16) During the 42nd Parliament (2015–2019), the Senate attempted to amend 13 government bills, whereas during the 41st Parliament (2011–2015), it had attempted to amend only one government bill.[[16]](#cite_note-Thomas2019-16) The reformed Senate is noted as having proposed amendments on at least 20% of all legislation.[[20]](#cite_note-VandenBeukelCochraneGodbout2021-20)

###### Criticism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Independent_politician&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Criticism")]

Several observers and those involved with the Senate itself have criticized the Trudeau government for its attempted reforms, with most accusations centering around the belief that the new appointment process is biased towards those who are ideologically supportive of the Liberal Party's objectives. Remaining Conservative senators have accused the Independent Senators Group in particular as being "too quick to endorse bills from the Liberal government".[[16]](#cite_note-Thomas2019-16)

Supporting this claim, a 2021 study found that members of the Independent Senators Group voted in favor of legislation proposed by the incumbent Liberal government more consistently than any other group within the Senate, including those still formally aligned to the Liberal Party.[[20]](#cite_note-VandenBeukelCochraneGodbout2021-20) This was, however, among an overall trend in which all senators demonstrated lower levels of party loyalty, and as such its full implications are still unknown. The report also concluded that partisanship in Senate appointments was undeniably down when compared to the Senate prior to the reforms.[[20]](#cite_note-VandenBeukelCochraneGodbout2021-20) It is generally thought that it will only be possible to judge the success of the attempted reforms accurately when a non-Liberal government is elected to the House of Commons, at which point it can be observed if the noted trend in voting represents simple loyalty to the government, or loyalty to the Liberal Party.[[20]](#cite_note-VandenBeukelCochraneGodbout2021-20)[[18]](#cite_note-Macfarlane2021-18)

#### Provincial and territorial politics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Independent_politician&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Provincial and territorial politics")]

The territorial legislatures of the [Northwest Territories](/wiki/Northwest_Territories "Northwest Territories") and [Nunavut](/wiki/Nunavut "Nunavut") are [consensus governments](/wiki/Consensus_government "Consensus government") with no political parties. All members sit as independents. There are a few independent members of the other provincial and territorial legislatures, which are similar in principle to the federal House of Commons; for example, in the [2009 British Columbia general election](/wiki/2009_British_Columbia_general_election "2009 British Columbia general election"), independent candidate [Vicki Huntington](/wiki/Vicki_Huntington "Vicki Huntington") narrowly defeated incumbent [Attorney General](/wiki/Attorney_General_of_British_Columbia "Attorney General of British Columbia") [Wally Oppal](/wiki/Wally_Oppal "Wally Oppal") in [Delta South](/wiki/Delta_South "Delta South"). In the [2019 Newfoundland and Labrador general election](/wiki/2019_Newfoundland_and_Labrador_general_election "2019 Newfoundland and Labrador general election"), two independent candidates were elected.[[21]](#cite_note-21)

### Costa Rica

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Independent_politician&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Costa Rica")]

Current laws in [Costa Rica](/wiki/Costa_Rica "Costa Rica") do not permit a citizen to run directly for any elected position as an independent without the representation of a political party.[[22]](#cite_note-regAL-22)[[23]](#cite_note-contecAL-23) Any nomination must be made through a political party, due to the framework of the current legal system, in which the political parties have a monopoly on the nomination of candidates for elected positions according to the Electoral Code.[[24]](#cite_note-códigoelectoral-24)

However, becoming an independent politician after being elected is protected by virtue of Article 25 of the [Constitution of Costa Rica](/wiki/Constitution_of_Costa_Rica "Constitution of Costa Rica"), which guarantees [freedom of association](/wiki/Freedom_of_association "Freedom of association"); therefore, any citizen cannot be forced to remain in a specific political party and can join any other political group. It is common in each legislative period for some [deputies](/wiki/Deputy_(legislator) "Deputy (legislator)") (*diputados*, term used for legislators) of the [Legislative Assembly of Costa Rica](/wiki/Legislative_Assembly_of_Costa_Rica "Legislative Assembly of Costa Rica") to become independents; this has also happened with the [mayors](/wiki/Mayor "Mayor") (*alcaldes*) of the municipalities of [cantons](/wiki/Cantons_of_Costa_Rica "Cantons of Costa Rica").[[25]](#cite_note-25)

### Mexico

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Independent_politician&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Mexico")]

[Jaime Heliodoro Rodríguez Calderón](/wiki/Jaime_Rodr%C3%ADguez_Calder%C3%B3n "Jaime Rodríguez Calderón") (born in 1957), sometimes referred to by his nickname "Bronco", is a Mexican politician and former governor for the northern state of [Nuevo León](/wiki/Nuevo_Le%C3%B3n "Nuevo León") and holds no political party affiliation. As of June 7, 2015[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Independent_politician&action=edit), he was elected Governor of Nuevo León, making history as the first independent candidate to win in the country.

#### President

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Independent_politician&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: President")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d6/Gilbert_Stuart_Williamstown_Portrait_of_George_Washington_%28cropped%29%282%29.jpg/250px-Gilbert_Stuart_Williamstown_Portrait_of_George_Washington_%28cropped%29%282%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Gilbert_Stuart_Williamstown_Portrait_of_George_Washington_(cropped)(2).jpg)

[George Washington](/wiki/George_Washington "George Washington"), the first [president of the United States](/wiki/President_of_the_United_States "President of the United States"), was an independent politician.

[George Washington](/wiki/George_Washington "George Washington") is the only [U.S. president](/wiki/President_of_the_United_States "President of the United States") elected as an independent to date. Washington opposed the development of [political parties](/wiki/Political_parties_in_the_United_States "Political parties in the United States"), which had begun to solidify as the [Federalist](/wiki/Federalist_Party "Federalist Party") faction, centered around [John Adams](/wiki/John_Adams "John Adams") and [Alexander Hamilton](/wiki/Alexander_Hamilton "Alexander Hamilton"), and the [Democratic-Republican](/wiki/Democratic-Republican_Party "Democratic-Republican Party") faction, centered around [Thomas Jefferson](/wiki/Thomas_Jefferson "Thomas Jefferson") and [James Madison](/wiki/James_Madison "James Madison"). Washington feared that [partisanship](/wiki/Partisan_(politics) "Partisan (politics)") would eventually destroy the country,[[26]](#cite_note-26) and famously warned against "the baneful effects of the spirit of party" in his 1796 [Farewell Address](/wiki/George_Washington%27s_Farewell_Address "George Washington's Farewell Address").[[27]](#cite_note-27)

[John Tyler](/wiki/John_Tyler "John Tyler") was expelled from the [Whig Party](/wiki/Whig_Party_(United_States) "Whig Party (United States)") in September 1841, and mostly remained an independent for the remainder of his presidency. He later briefly sought election in [1844](/wiki/1844_United_States_presidential_election "1844 United States presidential election") under the [Tyler Party](/wiki/Tyler_Party "Tyler Party"), but withdrew over fear he would split the vote and give the election to Whig candidate [Henry Clay](/wiki/Henry_Clay "Henry Clay").

Since 1900, candidates running as independents for U.S. president have included congressman [John Anderson](/wiki/John_B._Anderson "John B. Anderson") in [1980](/wiki/1980_United_States_presidential_election "1980 United States presidential election"), billionaire entrepreneur [Ross Perot](/wiki/Ross_Perot "Ross Perot") in [1992](/wiki/1992_United_States_presidential_election "1992 United States presidential election") and [1996](/wiki/United_States_third_party_and_independent_presidential_candidates,_1996 "United States third party and independent presidential candidates, 1996") (in 1996 under the newly founded [Reform Party](/wiki/Reform_Party_of_the_United_States_of_America "Reform Party of the United States of America")), former [Green Party](/wiki/Green_Party_of_the_United_States "Green Party of the United States") candidate [Ralph Nader](/wiki/Ralph_Nader "Ralph Nader") in the [1996](/wiki/United_States_third_party_and_independent_presidential_candidates,_1996 "United States third party and independent presidential candidates, 1996") and [2000](/wiki/United_States_third_party_and_independent_presidential_candidates,_2000 "United States third party and independent presidential candidates, 2000") elections, and ["Never Trump"](/wiki/Never_Trump_movement "Never Trump movement") conservative candidate [Evan McMullin](/wiki/Evan_McMullin "Evan McMullin") in [2016](/wiki/2016_United_States_presidential_election "2016 United States presidential election"). Out of all independent candidates since Washington, Perot performed the best, gaining no votes in the [Electoral College](/wiki/United_States_Electoral_College "United States Electoral College") but receiving 19 percent of the popular vote and, early in the election season, leading in polls against his opponents [Bill Clinton](/wiki/Bill_Clinton "Bill Clinton") and [George H. W. Bush](/wiki/George_H._W._Bush "George H. W. Bush").[[28]](#cite_note-28)[[29]](#cite_note-29)[[30]](#cite_note-30) Additionally, McMullin received 21 percent of the popular vote in his home state of [Utah](/wiki/Utah "Utah") but received little support from the remainder of the country.[[31]](#cite_note-31) Independent senator [Bernie Sanders](/wiki/Bernie_Sanders "Bernie Sanders") ran in the [2016](/wiki/2016_Democratic_Party_presidential_primaries "2016 Democratic Party presidential primaries") and [2020 Democratic Party presidential primaries](/wiki/2020_Democratic_Party_presidential_primaries "2020 Democratic Party presidential primaries"), but ultimately did not appear on the ballot in either the [2016](/wiki/2016_United_States_presidential_election "2016 United States presidential election") nor [2020](/wiki/2020_United_States_presidential_election "2020 United States presidential election") presidential elections, though he did receive [more than 5%](/wiki/2016_United_States_presidential_election_in_Vermont "2016 United States presidential election in Vermont") of the popular vote as a [write-in candidate](/wiki/Write-in_candidate "Write-in candidate") in his home state of [Vermont](/wiki/Vermont "Vermont").[[32]](#cite_note-32)

In [2008](/wiki/2008_United_States_presidential_election "2008 United States presidential election"), Nader formed Independent Parties in [New Mexico](/wiki/New_Mexico "New Mexico"), [Delaware](/wiki/Delaware "Delaware"), and elsewhere to gain ballot access in several states. In [2024](/wiki/2024_United_States_presidential_election "2024 United States presidential election"), [Robert F. Kennedy Jr.](/wiki/Robert_F._Kennedy_Jr. "Robert F. Kennedy Jr.") created "[We the People](/wiki/We_the_People_Party_(United_States) "We the People Party (United States)")" parties in several states for the same purpose, even though he is Independent.[[33]](#cite_note-33)[[34]](#cite_note-34)

Several other candidates for federal races, including [Joe Lieberman](/wiki/Joe_Lieberman "Joe Lieberman") (who created [Connecticut for Lieberman](/wiki/Connecticut_for_Lieberman "Connecticut for Lieberman")), have pursued a similar strategy.

#### Governor

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Independent_politician&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Governor")]

[Illinois](/wiki/Illinois "Illinois"), [Maine](/wiki/Maine "Maine"), [Oregon](/wiki/Oregon "Oregon"), [Rhode Island](/wiki/Rhode_Island "Rhode Island"), [Texas](/wiki/Texas "Texas"), [Alaska](/wiki/Alaska "Alaska"), and [North Dakota](/wiki/North_Dakota "North Dakota") have elected formally independent candidates as governor: Illinois's first two governors, [Shadrach Bond](/wiki/Shadrach_Bond "Shadrach Bond") and [Edward Coles](/wiki/Edward_Coles "Edward Coles"); [James B. Longley](/wiki/James_B._Longley "James B. Longley") in [1974](/wiki/1974_Maine_gubernatorial_election "1974 Maine gubernatorial election") as well as [Angus King](/wiki/Angus_King "Angus King") in [1994](/wiki/1994_Maine_gubernatorial_election "1994 Maine gubernatorial election") and [1998](/wiki/1998_Maine_gubernatorial_election "1998 Maine gubernatorial election") from Maine; [Lincoln Chafee](/wiki/Lincoln_Chafee "Lincoln Chafee") in [2010](/wiki/2010_Rhode_Island_gubernatorial_election "2010 Rhode Island gubernatorial election") from Rhode Island; [Julius Meier](/wiki/Julius_Meier "Julius Meier") in [1930](/wiki/1930_Oregon_gubernatorial_election "1930 Oregon gubernatorial election") from Oregon; [Sam Houston](/wiki/Sam_Houston "Sam Houston") in [1859](/wiki/1859_Texas_gubernatorial_election "1859 Texas gubernatorial election") from Texas; and [Bill Walker](/wiki/Bill_Walker_(American_politician) "Bill Walker (American politician)") in [2014](/wiki/2014_Alaska_gubernatorial_election "2014 Alaska gubernatorial election") from Alaska. [Lowell P. Weicker Jr.](/wiki/Lowell_Weicker "Lowell Weicker") of [Connecticut](/wiki/Connecticut "Connecticut") is sometimes mentioned as an independent governor, though this is not technically correct; he ran as an [A Connecticut Party](/wiki/A_Connecticut_Party "A Connecticut Party") candidate (which gave him better ballot placement than an unaffiliated candidate would receive), defeating the Democratic and Republican nominees. Another former governor who is sometimes mentioned as an independent is [Jesse Ventura](/wiki/Jesse_Ventura "Jesse Ventura"), who actually ran as a member of the [Reform Party](/wiki/Reform_Party_of_the_United_States_of_America "Reform Party of the United States of America")'s [Minnesota](/wiki/Minnesota "Minnesota") affiliate, which later disaffiliated from the party and reverted to its original name, the [Independence Party of Minnesota](/wiki/Independence_Party_of_Minnesota "Independence Party of Minnesota"). North Dakota elected William Langer Governor in 1933 as a member of the Republican/Nonpartisan League. He was later elected Governor in 1937 as an Independent.

In [1971](/wiki/1971_Virginia_lieutenant_gubernatorial_special_election "1971 Virginia lieutenant gubernatorial special election"), State Senator [Henry Howell](/wiki/Henry_Howell "Henry Howell") of [Virginia](/wiki/Virginia "Virginia"), a former Democrat, was elected [lieutenant governor](/wiki/Lieutenant_Governor_of_Virginia "Lieutenant Governor of Virginia") as an independent. [Two years later](/wiki/1973_Virginia_gubernatorial_election "1973 Virginia gubernatorial election"), he campaigned for [governor](/wiki/Governor_of_Virginia "Governor of Virginia") as an independent, but lost by 15,000 votes.

There were several unsuccessful independent gubernatorial candidates in 2006 who impacted their electoral races. In [Maine](/wiki/2006_Maine_gubernatorial_election "2006 Maine gubernatorial election"), state legislator [Barbara Merrill](/wiki/Barbara_Merrill "Barbara Merrill") (formerly a Democrat) received 21% of the vote. In [Texas](/wiki/2006_Texas_gubernatorial_election "2006 Texas gubernatorial election"), [country music](/wiki/Country_music "Country music") singer and mystery novelist [Kinky Friedman](/wiki/Kinky_Friedman "Kinky Friedman") received 12.43% of the vote, and State Comptroller [Carole Keeton Strayhorn](/wiki/Carole_Keeton_Strayhorn "Carole Keeton Strayhorn") received 18.13%. Strayhorn and Friedman's presence in the race resulted in a splitting of the ballot four ways between themselves and the two major parties.

In [2010, Florida](/wiki/2010_United_States_Senate_election_in_Florida "2010 United States Senate election in Florida") governor [Charlie Crist](/wiki/Charlie_Crist "Charlie Crist") left the Republican party and became an independent. (He later became a Democrat.)[[35]](#cite_note-35) He left the Republicans because he did not want to run against former state house Speaker [Marco Rubio](/wiki/Marco_Rubio "Marco Rubio") in the Republican primary for the U.S. Senate election, preferring to run in the general election instead. Rubio won the election, though Crist came in ahead of Democratic nominee [Kendrick Meek](/wiki/Kendrick_Meek "Kendrick Meek").

In [2014](/wiki/2014_Hawaii_gubernatorial_election "2014 Hawaii gubernatorial election"), former [Honolulu](/wiki/Honolulu "Honolulu") mayor [Mufi Hannemann](/wiki/Mufi_Hannemann "Mufi Hannemann") ran as an independent candidate for the [governorship](/wiki/Governor_of_Hawaii "Governor of Hawaii") of the State of [Hawaii](/wiki/Hawaii "Hawaii") after previously campaigning in the state's Democratic primary. As a result, Democratic candidate [David Ige](/wiki/David_Ige "David Ige") was elected as governor with a plurality of 49%.[[36]](#cite_note-36) Also in 2014, former mayor of [Valdez](/wiki/Valdez,_Alaska "Valdez, Alaska"), [Bill Walker](/wiki/Bill_Walker_(American_politician) "Bill Walker (American politician)") won the gubernatorial election. Walker retired before the [2018 election](/wiki/2018_Alaska_gubernatorial_election "2018 Alaska gubernatorial election") but ran again in the [2022](/wiki/2022_Alaska_gubernatorial_election "2022 Alaska gubernatorial election"). He did not win but received 20% of the vote.

#### Congress

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Independent_politician&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Congress")]

##### House of Representatives

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Independent_politician&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: House of Representatives")]

The [United States House of Representatives](/wiki/United_States_House_of_Representatives "United States House of Representatives") has also seen a handful of [independent members](/wiki/Third-party_and_independent_members_of_the_United_States_Congress "Third-party and independent members of the United States Congress"). Examples include [Bernie Sanders](/wiki/Bernie_Sanders "Bernie Sanders") of Vermont, [Virgil Goode](/wiki/Virgil_Goode "Virgil Goode") of Virginia, [Frazier Reams](/wiki/Frazier_Reams "Frazier Reams") of [Ohio](/wiki/Ohio "Ohio"), [Victor Berger](/wiki/Victor_L._Berger "Victor L. Berger") of [Wisconsin](/wiki/Wisconsin "Wisconsin"), and [Justin Amash](/wiki/Justin_Amash "Justin Amash") and [Paul Mitchell](/wiki/Paul_Mitchell_(politician) "Paul Mitchell (politician)") of [Michigan](/wiki/Michigan "Michigan").

##### Senate

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Independent_politician&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Senate")]

There have been several independents elected to the [United States Senate](/wiki/United_States_Senate "United States Senate") throughout history. Notable examples include [David Davis](/wiki/David_Davis_(Supreme_Court_justice) "David Davis (Supreme Court justice)") of Illinois (a former [Republican](/wiki/Republican_Party_(United_States) "Republican Party (United States)")) in the 19th century, and [Harry F. Byrd Jr.](/wiki/Harry_F._Byrd_Jr. "Harry F. Byrd Jr.") of Virginia (who had been elected to his first term as a Democrat) in the 20th century. Some officials have been elected as members of a party but became independent while in office (without being elected as such), such as [Wayne Morse](/wiki/Wayne_Morse "Wayne Morse") of Oregon, who left the Republican party to become an independent, then joined the Democratic Party two years later. [Nebraska](/wiki/Nebraska "Nebraska") senator [George W. Norris](/wiki/George_W._Norris "George W. Norris") was elected for four terms as a Republican before changing to an independent after the Republicans lost their majority in Congress in 1930. Norris won re-election as an independent in 1936, but later lost his final re-election attempt to Republican [Kenneth S. Wherry](/wiki/Kenneth_S._Wherry "Kenneth S. Wherry") in 1942.

###### Since 2000

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Independent_politician&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Since 2000")]

Vermont senator [Jim Jeffords](/wiki/Jim_Jeffords "Jim Jeffords") left the Republican Party to become an independent in 2001. Jeffords's [change of party status](/wiki/Party_switching "Party switching") was especially significant because it shifted the Senate composition from 50 to 50 between the Republicans and Democrats (with a Republican Vice President, [Dick Cheney](/wiki/Dick_Cheney "Dick Cheney"), who would presumably break all ties in favor of the Republicans), to 49 Republicans, 50 Democrats, and one Independent. Jeffords agreed to vote for Democratic control of the Senate in exchange for being appointed chairman of the [Senate Environment and Public Works Committee](/wiki/United_States_Senate_Committee_on_Environment_and_Public_Works "United States Senate Committee on Environment and Public Works"), and the Democrats held control of the Senate until the [2002 elections](/wiki/2002_United_States_Senate_elections "2002 United States Senate elections"), when the Republicans regained their majority. Jeffords retired at the end of his term in 2007. [Dean Barkley](/wiki/Dean_Barkley "Dean Barkley") of the [Independence Party of Minnesota](/wiki/Independence_Party_of_Minnesota "Independence Party of Minnesota") was appointed a day before the 2002 elections to fill the senate seat of [Paul Wellstone](/wiki/Paul_Wellstone "Paul Wellstone") who, while running for re-election, died weeks prior. Barkley refused to caucus with either party.

In [2006](/wiki/2006_United_States_Senate_election_in_Vermont "2006 United States Senate election in Vermont"), independent politician [Bernie Sanders](/wiki/Bernie_Sanders "Bernie Sanders") won the Senate seat vacated by the retiring Jim Jeffords as an independent and was reelected in [2012](/wiki/2012_United_States_Senate_election_in_Vermont "2012 United States Senate election in Vermont"), [2018](/wiki/2018_United_States_Senate_election_in_Vermont "2018 United States Senate election in Vermont") and [2024](/wiki/2024_United_States_Senate_election_in_Vermont "2024 United States Senate election in Vermont"). He was an independent member of the [United States House of Representatives](/wiki/United_States_House_of_Representatives "United States House of Representatives") for [Vermont-at-large](/wiki/Vermont%27s_at-large_congressional_district "Vermont's at-large congressional district") from 1991 to 2007. Sanders is the longest-serving independent member of Congress in American history.[[37]](#cite_note-37) Also in [2006](/wiki/2006_United_States_Senate_election_in_Connecticut "2006 United States Senate election in Connecticut"), [Joe Lieberman](/wiki/Joe_Lieberman "Joe Lieberman") was a former [Democrat](/wiki/Democratic_Party_(United_States) "Democratic Party (United States)") and ran under a third party ([Connecticut for Lieberman Party](/wiki/Connecticut_for_Lieberman "Connecticut for Lieberman")) after he lost the primary. After the election, Lieberman enrolled himself as an [Independent Democrat](/wiki/Independent_Democrat "Independent Democrat") until his retirement in 2013. In 2006, Sanders and Lieberman were the only two victorious independent candidates for Congress, both caucusing with the Democrats. In [2012](/wiki/2012_United_States_Senate_election_in_Maine "2012 United States Senate election in Maine"), [Angus King](/wiki/Angus_King "Angus King") was elected to the U.S. Senate as an Independent from Maine. He was reelected in [2018](/wiki/2018_United_States_Senate_election_in_Maine "2018 United States Senate election in Maine") and won a third term in [2024](/wiki/2024_United_States_Senate_election_in_Maine "2024 United States Senate election in Maine").

During the [presidency of Joe Biden](/wiki/Presidency_of_Joe_Biden "Presidency of Joe Biden"), former members of Democratic Party [Kyrsten Sinema](/wiki/Kyrsten_Sinema "Kyrsten Sinema") and [Joe Manchin](/wiki/Joe_Manchin "Joe Manchin") joined Sanders and King and became [Independent Democrats](/wiki/Independent_Democrat "Independent Democrat"). They are viewed as [moderate Democrats](/wiki/New_Democrats_(United_States) "New Democrats (United States)") and cited increasing partisanship to explain their decisions.[[38]](#cite_note-38)[[39]](#cite_note-39) After they changed their affiliations, the Senate had the highest number of independents in a single Congress since the ratification of the [17th Amendment](/wiki/Seventeenth_Amendment_to_the_United_States_Constitution "Seventeenth Amendment to the United States Constitution").[[40]](#cite_note-40)

**State and local independent politicians**

Independent politicians have also played notable roles at the state and local levels, often finding success in contexts where party affiliation is less dominant or elections are nonpartisan.[[41]](#cite_note-41)

**Mayors and municipal leaders**

There have been many cases where independent candidates have made a large impact on elections and have even won, particularly in large cities. For instance, former New York City mayor [Michael Bloomberg](/wiki/Michael_Bloomberg "Michael Bloomberg") served as 2002 to 2013 as a Republican but for his third term he won as an independent.[[42]](#cite_note-42) Independent politicians have also led cities like [Minneapolis](/wiki/Minneapolis "Minneapolis"), where [Charles Stenvig](/wiki/Charles_Stenvig "Charles Stenvig") served as an independent mayor throughout the 1970s.[[43]](#cite_note-43) These leaders often emphasize pragmatic governance over party ideology, appealing to diverse voter bases.

**State legislatures**

While less common than in Congress, independents occasionally serve in state legislatures. [Maine](/wiki/Maine_Legislature "Maine Legislature") and [Alaska](/wiki/Alaska_State_Legislature "Alaska State Legislature"), in particular, have seen a history of independent state legislators. For example, in the [Alaska State House](/wiki/Alaska_House_of_Representatives "Alaska House of Representatives"), independents have sometimes played pivotal roles in coalition governments, demonstrating their influence in closely divided chambers. These legislators often prioritize regional or policy-specific issues over strict adherence to party platforms.[[44]](#cite_note-44)

**Judicial and nonpartisan offices**

Many state and local offices, particularly in the judiciary, are officially nonpartisan, providing opportunities for independents to succeed.[[45]](#cite_note-45) For example, state superintendents of schools or city council members are normally politicians who identify as independent or with no party at all. This system allows voters to focus on the candidate's qualifications rather than party affiliation since this should not be stressed for these positions.[[46]](#cite_note-46)

## Asia

### China

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Independent_politician&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: China")]

#### Hong Kong

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Independent_politician&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Hong Kong")]

Around half of [Hong Kong's Legislative Council](/wiki/Legislative_Council_of_Hong_Kong "Legislative Council of Hong Kong") is made up of independents, or members whose political groups are represented by one sole member in the legislature. They are common in [functional constituencies](/wiki/Functional_constituency_(Hong_Kong) "Functional constituency (Hong Kong)"), and are not rare among [geographical constituencies](/wiki/Geographical_constituency "Geographical constituency").

### India

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Independent_politician&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: India")]

Independent candidates can contest elections on the basis of their personal appeal or to promote an ideology different from any party. In the [2024 general election](/wiki/2024_Indian_general_election "2024 Indian general election"), seven independent candidates were elected to [Lok Sabha](/wiki/Lok_Sabha "Lok Sabha"), the lower house of the [Parliament of India](/wiki/Parliament_of_India "Parliament of India").[[47]](#cite_note-47)[[48]](#cite_note-48)[[49]](#cite_note-49)

### Malaysia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Independent_politician&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Malaysia")]

Independents have rarely been elected to the [Dewan Rakyat](/wiki/Dewan_Rakyat "Dewan Rakyat") and state legislative assemblies. In Malaysian elections, many independent candidates lose their election deposit because they had failed to secure at least 12.5% or one-eighth of the total votes cast. Since [senators](/wiki/List_of_members_of_the_Dewan_Negara "List of members of the Dewan Negara") in Malaysia are only appointed with the guidance of the prime minister by either the [King of Malaysia](/wiki/King_of_Malaysia "King of Malaysia") or the respective state assemblies, independent [senators](/wiki/List_of_members_of_the_Dewan_Negara "List of members of the Dewan Negara") are quite rare, with most of them being technocrats or niche experts whose function is to serve a ministry.

In 2010, a group of independent MPs who were sacked from the [People's Justice Party](/wiki/People%27s_Justice_Party_(Malaysia) "People's Justice Party (Malaysia)") formed a political block called *[Konsensus Bebas](https://ms.wikipedia.org/wiki/Konsensus_Bebas "ms:Konsensus Bebas")*.[[50]](#cite_note-50) The members were [Zahrain Mohamed Hashim](/wiki/Zahrain_Mohamed_Hashim "Zahrain Mohamed Hashim") (Bayan Baru), [Wee Choo Keong](/wiki/Wee_Choo_Keong "Wee Choo Keong") (Wangsa Maju), [Zulkifli Noordin](/wiki/Zulkifli_Noordin "Zulkifli Noordin") (Kulim-Bandar Bharu), [Tan Tee Beng](/wiki/Tan_Tee_Beng "Tan Tee Beng") (Nibong Tebal) and [Mohsin Fadzli Samsuri](/wiki/Mohsin_Fadzli_Samsuri "Mohsin Fadzli Samsuri") (Bagan Serai). It did not last beyond the 12th General Elections.

As of May 2018[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Independent_politician&action=edit), three independent MPs were elected in [GE14](/wiki/Malaysian_General_Election "Malaysian General Election"), but later joined [Pakatan Harapan](/wiki/Pakatan_Harapan "Pakatan Harapan") ([PKR](/wiki/People%27s_Justice_Party_(Malaysia) "People's Justice Party (Malaysia)")), thus causing no representation for independent MP for that time. However, as of June 2018 and December 2018, the number increased to 13 independent Members of Parliament that sat in the [Dewan Rakyat](/wiki/Dewan_Rakyat "Dewan Rakyat") as of December 2018. At the same time in December 2018, almost all members from Sabah [UMNO](/wiki/UMNO "UMNO") quit the party and became independent politicians.

### North Korea

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Independent_politician&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: North Korea")]

Parliamentary independent candidates: The system in place whither the DPRK allows for independent politicians to launch their own campaigns to gain a seat in parliament. The candidates however must be approved by the Fatherland Front, being the primary party of the DPRK. To cast votes to independent candidates the voting population must do so at independent voting stations.

Nearly all electoral systems currently in practice in the DPRK that exist on a local level are made up of mostly independent Candidates, as the Fatherland Front and other major party's primarily operate in the urban heartland of the DPRK. On the local level of North Korean elections, alliances between independent candidates is banned.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Nepal

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Independent_politician&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: Nepal")]

In Nepal, there are some independent politicians, especially in local government. Independent politician and rapper [Balen Shah](/wiki/Balendra_Shah "Balendra Shah") was elected as Mayor of [Kathmandu](/wiki/Kathmandu "Kathmandu") in 2022.[[51]](#cite_note-51)[[52]](#cite_note-52) Similarly, [Harka Sampang](/wiki/Harka_Raj_Rai "Harka Raj Rai") and [Gopal Hamal](/wiki/Gopal_Hamal "Gopal Hamal") were also elected as Mayor of some of the major cities like [Dharan](/wiki/Dharan "Dharan") and [Dhangadhi](/wiki/Dhangadhi "Dhangadhi"), respectively.[[53]](#cite_note-53)[[54]](#cite_note-54)

### Pakistan

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Independent_politician&action=edit&section=35 "Edit section: Pakistan")]

Pakistan has independent politicians standing in elections. In the [2008 Pakistani general election](/wiki/2008_Pakistani_general_election "2008 Pakistani general election"), 30 independent members of parliament were elected to [Pakistan's parliament](/wiki/Pakistan%27s_parliament "Pakistan's parliament"). In 2011, four candidates won seats in the [National Assembly](/wiki/National_Assembly_of_Pakistan "National Assembly of Pakistan"). In the 2013 General Election, nine seats were won by independents. In 2024, [Pakistan Tehreek-e-Insaf](/wiki/Pakistan_Tehreek-e-Insaf "Pakistan Tehreek-e-Insaf") backed independent candidates won 94 seats as the party was officially banned by the [Election Commission of Pakistan](/wiki/Election_Commission_of_Pakistan "Election Commission of Pakistan").

Ever since the first elections during the [1907 Philippine Assembly elections](/wiki/1907_Philippine_Assembly_elections "1907 Philippine Assembly elections"), independents have been allowed to participate and have won seats. On that first election, independents had the most members, behind the [Nacionalista Party](/wiki/Nacionalista_Party "Nacionalista Party"). When the [Senate](/wiki/Senate_of_the_Philippines "Senate of the Philippines") was first created, its [first elections in 1916](/wiki/1916_Philippine_Senate_elections "1916 Philippine Senate elections") also saw independents participating and winning one seat. In the Nacionalista landslide of [1941](/wiki/1941_Philippine_general_election "1941 Philippine general election"), the three independents were the only non-members of the Nacionalista Party to win in the House of Representatives; this was also the start of independents being shut out in the Senate.

After independence was granted by the United States in 1946, the [two-party system](/wiki/Two-party_system "Two-party system") between the Nacionalistas and [Liberal Party](/wiki/Liberal_Party_(Philippines) "Liberal Party (Philippines)") was established, with certain candidates who failed to get the nomination of either parties appearing on the ballot as "Independent Nacionalista" or "Independent Liberal", as the case may be. Independents not associated with any party were still able to participate and sporadically win elections. In the [1961 Philippine vice-presidential election](/wiki/1961_Philippine_presidential_election "1961 Philippine presidential election"), independent [Sergio Osmeña Jr.](/wiki/Sergio_Osme%C3%B1a_Jr. "Sergio Osmeña Jr.") narrowly lost to [Emmanuel Pelaez](/wiki/Emmanuel_Pelaez "Emmanuel Pelaez"). The first breakthrough was in the [1967 Philippine Senate election](/wiki/1967_Philippine_Senate_election "1967 Philippine Senate election") where [Magnolia Antonino](/wiki/Magnolia_Antonino "Magnolia Antonino"), widow of Gaudencio Antonino who died on election eve, won.

[Ferdinand Marcos](/wiki/Ferdinand_Marcos "Ferdinand Marcos") declared martial law in 1972, dissolved Congress, and promulgated a new constitution that led to the [1978 Philippine parliamentary election](/wiki/1978_Philippine_parliamentary_election "1978 Philippine parliamentary election"), where one independent won, independents also won in [1984](/wiki/1984_Philippine_parliamentary_election "1984 Philippine parliamentary election"). Marcos was overthrown after the 1986 [People Power Revolution](/wiki/People_Power_Revolution "People Power Revolution") after he allegedly cheated in the [1986 Philippine presidential election](/wiki/1986_Philippine_presidential_election "1986 Philippine presidential election"). [Corazon Aquino](/wiki/Corazon_Aquino "Corazon Aquino") succeeded Marcos, and promulgated a new constitution that ushered in a [multi-party system](/wiki/Multi-party_system "Multi-party system"). Here, parties are not able to present full 12-person slates in Senate elections, thus necessitating inter-party cooperation, that included independents. The [1995 Philippine Senate election](/wiki/1995_Philippine_Senate_election "1995 Philippine Senate election") saw two independents winning: [Juan Ponce Enrile](/wiki/Juan_Ponce_Enrile "Juan Ponce Enrile") (who later ran and lost the [1998 Philippine presidential election](/wiki/1998_Philippine_presidential_election "1998 Philippine presidential election") as an independent) and [Gregorio Honasan](/wiki/Gregorio_Honasan "Gregorio Honasan"), who both teamed up to stage coups during the Aquino presidency. The [2001 EDSA Revolution](/wiki/2001_EDSA_Revolution "2001 EDSA Revolution") increased the number of major candidates running as independents, with broadcaster [Noli de Castro](/wiki/Noli_de_Castro "Noli de Castro") topping the Senate election. He was a guest candidate of the opposition [Pwersa ng Masa coalition](/wiki/Pwersa_ng_Masang_Pilipino "Pwersa ng Masang Pilipino") but he never joined their campaign rallies. In 2004, he ran [as vice president](/wiki/2004_Philippine_presidential_election "2004 Philippine presidential election") as a guest candidate of the administration [K-4 coalition](/wiki/Koalisyon_ng_Katapatan_at_Karanasan_sa_Kinabukasan "Koalisyon ng Katapatan at Karanasan sa Kinabukasan") and won with just under majority of the vote.

In the local level, former priest [Eddie Panlilio](/wiki/Eddie_Panlilio "Eddie Panlilio") was elected as governor of [Pampanga](/wiki/Pampanga "Pampanga") in 2007, defeating two administration candidates. When Panlilio eventually transferred to the Liberal Party in time for the 2010 election, it was ruled that he was beaten in the 2007 election; in 2010, he was defeated.

In the [2010 House of Representatives elections](/wiki/2010_Philippine_House_of_Representatives_elections "2010 Philippine House of Representatives elections"), seven independents were elected, although all but two joined a political party after the elections. Independents can only run in [district](/wiki/Congressional_districts_of_the_Philippines "Congressional districts of the Philippines") elections, and cannot participate in [party-list elections](/wiki/Party-list_representation_in_the_House_of_Representatives_of_the_Philippines "Party-list representation in the House of Representatives of the Philippines") as independents.

In contesting elections, independent candidates can spend as much as those with parties can under the law, but they are not able to tap in spending from a political party that nominated them.

Independent candidates are different from [nonpartisan](/wiki/Nonpartisanism "Nonpartisanism") politicians; the former are elected in openly partisan elections, while the latter participate in nonpartisan elections such as [barangay elections](/wiki/Barangay_elections "Barangay elections"). Local legislatures may find itself with independent and nonpartisan members.

In Qatar, political parties are banned by law. All elected officials are independent.[[55]](#cite_note-55)

### Bulgaria

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Independent_politician&action=edit&section=41 "Edit section: Bulgaria")]

The [President of Bulgaria](/wiki/President_of_Bulgaria "President of Bulgaria") [Rumen Radev](/wiki/Rumen_Radev "Rumen Radev") is an independent with support from the [Bulgarian Socialist Party](/wiki/Bulgarian_Socialist_Party "Bulgarian Socialist Party"). Radev was elected in the [2016 presidential election](/wiki/2016_Bulgarian_presidential_election "2016 Bulgarian presidential election"). However, they can be part of a civic quota of a given party. ### Finland

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Independent_politician&action=edit&section=44 "Edit section: Finland")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/01/Carl_Gustaf_Emil_Mannerheim_1940.jpg/250px-Carl_Gustaf_Emil_Mannerheim_1940.jpg)](/wiki/File:Carl_Gustaf_Emil_Mannerheim_1940.jpg)

Marshal Mannerheim, the 6th president of Finland

[Marshal](/wiki/Marshal_of_Finland "Marshal of Finland") [C. G. E. Mannerheim](/wiki/C._G._E._Mannerheim "C. G. E. Mannerheim"), who served as the [president of Finland](/wiki/President_of_Finland "President of Finland") from 1944 to 1946, did not want to be affiliated with any party.[[58]](#cite_note-58) As the state regent/caretaker from December 1918 to July 1919, Mannerheim also stood as an independent in the [July 1919 presidential election](/wiki/1919_Finnish_presidential_election "1919 Finnish presidential election") against the [National Progressive's](/wiki/National_Progressive_Party_(Finland) "National Progressive Party (Finland)") candidate [Kaarlo Juho Ståhlberg](/wiki/Kaarlo_Juho_St%C3%A5hlberg "Kaarlo Juho Ståhlberg"), who won.

Also, after serving six years on his first term as the 12th president of Finland in the [National Coalition Party](/wiki/National_Coalition_Party "National Coalition Party") from 2012 to 2018, [Sauli Niinistö](/wiki/Sauli_Niinist%C3%B6 "Sauli Niinistö") was elected for his second term in 2018 after running as an independent candidate. Sauli Niinistö's status as an independent/non-partisan president has been attributed to his historical approval ratings and popularity, which stood at 90% favorable in July 2021 of which 52% said that Niinistö had handled the presidency "Very favorably".[[59]](#cite_note-59)[[60]](#cite_note-60)

### France

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Independent_politician&action=edit&section=45 "Edit section: France")]

In France, independent politicians are frequently categorised as *sans étiquette* ("without label") in municipal or district elections.

In the nineteenth century and first half of the twentieth century, most French national politicians were independents. The first modern French political parties date from the early 1900s (foundation of [Action Libérale](/wiki/Popular_Liberal_Action "Popular Liberal Action") and the [Radical Party](/wiki/Radical_Party_(France) "Radical Party (France)")). The first legislation on political parties dates from 1911, though it was not until 1928 that parliamentarians were required to select a political party for the parliamentary register (either by formally joining a group, or by loosely working with one as an *apparenté*, or associate), and not until after 1945 that structured political parties came to dominate parliamentary work.

Once elected, independents tended to attach themselves to a parliamentary party. In some cases independent deputies banded together to form a [technical group](/wiki/Technical_group "Technical group") of their own. In 1932, for instance, there were four technical groups created: the left-of-centre [Independent Left](/wiki/Independent_Left_(France) "Independent Left (France)"), with 12 deputies; the centre-right liberal [Independents of the Left](/wiki/Independents_of_the_Left "Independents of the Left"), with 26 deputies; the right-wing [agrarian](/wiki/Agrarianism "Agrarianism") [Independents for Economic, Social and Peasant Action](/wiki/Independents_for_Economic,_Social_and_Peasant_Action "Independents for Economic, Social and Peasant Action"), with six deputies; and the far-right [monarchist](/wiki/Monarchism_in_France "Monarchism in France") [Independent Group](/wiki/Republican_Independents "Republican Independents"), with 12 deputies—these four technical groups thus accounted for one-tenth of deputies. In addition, the larger parliamentary parties, including the socialist SFIO, centre-left PRRRS, centre-right ARD and conservative FR all included a greater or lesser number of independents who sat with their group for parliamentary work (*apparentés*).

In 1920, [Alexandre Millerand](/wiki/Alexandre_Millerand "Alexandre Millerand") was elected [president of the Republic](/wiki/President_of_France "President of France") under the banner "without label".

However, it is nowadays rare to have independent politicians at national level, if only because independents usually affiliate themselves to an existing political grouping. Noteworthy independents include [José Bové](/wiki/Jos%C3%A9_Bov%C3%A9 "José Bové") in the [2007 presidential election](/wiki/2007_French_presidential_election "2007 French presidential election"). [Emmanuel Macron](/wiki/Emmanuel_Macron "Emmanuel Macron") was an independent politician as Minister, but formed his own party to stand in the [2017 presidential election](/wiki/2017_French_presidential_election "2017 French presidential election").

From 2001 to 2008, "without label" was no longer used in the nomenclature of the [Ministry of the Interior](/wiki/Minister_of_the_Interior_(France) "Minister of the Interior (France)"). Candidates and lists presenting themselves as "without label" are classified in DVG (various left), DVD (various right), DVC (various center) or AUT (other) according to their political sensitivity. Therefore, from 2008 onwards, the DIV (miscellaneous) or the LDIV code for the "miscellaneous" list has been created to group unclassifiable or categorical interests and, by default, mayors without a declared label claiming no political sensitivity, be it left, center or right. The AUT (other) grade replaces the DIV grade without changing its definition.[[61]](#cite_note-61)

[Salome Zourabichvili](/wiki/Salome_Zourabichvili "Salome Zourabichvili") won the [2018 Georgian presidential election](/wiki/2018_Georgian_presidential_election "2018 Georgian presidential election") as an independent candidate, becoming the first-ever female [President of Georgia](/wiki/President_of_Georgia "President of Georgia").

### Germany

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Independent_politician&action=edit&section=47 "Edit section: Germany")]

[Joachim Gauck](/wiki/Joachim_Gauck "Joachim Gauck"), [President of Germany](/wiki/President_of_Germany "President of Germany") from March 2012 to March 2017 and the first Federal President without party affiliation, was to date the most prominent independent politician. In the [German presidential election of 2010](/wiki/2010_German_presidential_election "2010 German presidential election") he was the candidate of the [Social Democrats](/wiki/Social_Democratic_Party_of_Germany "Social Democratic Party of Germany") and [Greens](/wiki/Alliance_%2790/The_Greens "Alliance '90/The Greens"), in [2012](/wiki/2012_German_presidential_election "2012 German presidential election") the candidate of all major parties except [The Left](/wiki/The_Left_(Germany) "The Left (Germany)"). His presidency—though his powers are limited—constitutes an exception, as Independent politicians have rarely held high office in German history, at least not since [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"). It has nevertheless happened that a presidential candidate without any chances of election by the [Federal Convention](/wiki/Federal_Convention_(Germany) "Federal Convention (Germany)") was not a party member: for example, in 1984 the Greens came up with the writer [Luise Rinser](/wiki/Luise_Rinser "Luise Rinser").

In the [Bundestag](/wiki/Bundestag "Bundestag") parliament nearly all deputies belong to a political party. The voting system of [personalized proportional representation](/wiki/Mixed-member_proportional_representation "Mixed-member proportional representation") (since 1949) allows any individual holding the [passive right to vote](/wiki/Suffrage "Suffrage") to stand for a direct [mandate](/wiki/Mandate_(politics) "Mandate (politics)") in the [electoral districts](/wiki/Electoral_districts "Electoral districts")—299 of the seats in parliament are distributed by districts according to a [plurality voting system](/wiki/Plurality_voting_system "Plurality voting system"). Such a candidate has to present 200 signatures in favor of their candidacy, the same as a candidate of a party that had no parliamentary presentation previously. The first [Bundestag election in 1949](/wiki/1949_West_German_federal_election "1949 West German federal election") saw three independents elected; since then, no party-independent candidate has won a seat.[[62]](#cite_note-62) At [state](/wiki/States_of_Germany "States of Germany") level, the situation is more or less the same: only party members have a real chance to be elected to a [Landtag](/wiki/Landtag "Landtag") legislature, and state ministers without party membership are just as rare as at the federal level.

In [local elections](/wiki/Local_election "Local election") however it is common for independent politicians to be elected into [district](/wiki/Districts_of_Germany "Districts of Germany"), city and municipal councils; sometimes independent politicians are even elected as [mayor](/wiki/Mayor "Mayor") or *[Landrat](/wiki/Landrat "Landrat")*, especially in [Northern Germany](/wiki/Northern_Germany "Northern Germany"). In Germany it is common to form [free voters' associations](/wiki/Grouping_of_electors#Germany "Grouping of electors") to contest in local elections, often to greater success. An alliance of multiple of these voters' associations formed the [Free Voters](/wiki/Free_Voters "Free Voters") which later became a political party. The Free Voters have succeeded at entering two state parliaments directly: the [Landtag of Bavaria](/wiki/Landtag_of_Bavaria "Landtag of Bavaria") ([Free Voters of Bavaria](/wiki/Free_Voters_of_Bavaria "Free Voters of Bavaria")) in 2008 and the [Landtag of Rhineland-Palatinate](/wiki/Landtag_of_Rhineland-Palatinate "Landtag of Rhineland-Palatinate") in 2021. Another party associated with the Free Voters that made it into a state parliament was the [Brandenburg United Civic Movements/Free Voters](/wiki/Brandenburg_United_Civic_Movements/Free_Voters "Brandenburg United Civic Movements/Free Voters"), which first entered the [Landtag of Brandenburg](/wiki/Landtag_of_Brandenburg "Landtag of Brandenburg") with three seats in 2014 but lost all of its seats in 2024.

An independent member of parliament, who also is not a member of a [voters' association](/wiki/Grouping_of_electors#Germany "Grouping of electors"), holds the status of *fraktionsloser Abgeordneter*, i.e., not affiliated to any [parliamentary group](/wiki/Parliamentary_group "Parliamentary group"). A representative who either leaves their party (and their parliamentary group) or is expelled from it and does not join another becomes *fraktionslos*. In 1989 the Bundestag MP [Thomas Wüppesahl](/wiki/Thomas_W%C3%BCppesahl "Thomas Wüppesahl"), who had left the Green Party in 1987 and was excluded from the Green parliamentary group the next year, obtained more rights as a *fraktionsloser Abgeordneter*, for example more talking time and representation in a subcommittee, when the [Federal Constitutional Court](/wiki/Federal_Constitutional_Court_of_Germany "Federal Constitutional Court of Germany") decided partially in their favor.

After the [German unification](/wiki/Unification_of_Germany "Unification of Germany") of 1871, the first [Reich Chancellors](/wiki/Chancellor_of_Germany "Chancellor of Germany") ([heads of government](/wiki/Head_of_government "Head of government")) *de jure* served as [executive officers](/wiki/Executive_officer "Executive officer") of the [German Imperial states](/wiki/German_Empire "German Empire") as non-partisans, usually recruited from the traditional bureaucratic, aristocratic or military elites. In the fierce political conflicts during the [Weimar](/wiki/Weimar_Republic "Weimar Republic") period after [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I"), several chancellors and Reich Ministers also had no party affiliation: these chancellors were [Wilhelm Cuno](/wiki/Wilhelm_Cuno "Wilhelm Cuno") (1922–1923), [Hans Luther](/wiki/Hans_Luther "Hans Luther") (1925–1926), the former [Centre](/wiki/Centre_Party_(Germany) "Centre Party (Germany)") politician [Franz von Papen](/wiki/Franz_von_Papen "Franz von Papen") (1932), and [Kurt von Schleicher](/wiki/Kurt_von_Schleicher "Kurt von Schleicher") (1932–1933). The last two [cabinets](/wiki/Cabinet_of_Germany "Cabinet of Germany") appointed by Reich President [Paul von Hindenburg](/wiki/Paul_von_Hindenburg "Paul von Hindenburg"), a non-partisan (though strongly [Conservative](/wiki/Conservatism_in_Germany "Conservatism in Germany")) himself, were regarded as apolitical cabinets of experts with regard to the rise of the [Nazi Party](/wiki/Nazi_Party "Nazi Party"); many of the ministers were not party members.

Since World War II, only two ministers of (West) German cabinets have not been party members, though "on the ticket" of the major party in the coalition, the Social Democrats: Education Minister [Hans Leussink](/wiki/Hans_Leussink "Hans Leussink") (1969–1972), and Minister of Economy [Werner Müller](/wiki/Werner_M%C3%BCller_(politician) "Werner Müller (politician)") (1998–2002). Minister of Justice [Klaus Kinkel](/wiki/Klaus_Kinkel "Klaus Kinkel") only shortly after his appointment joined the [Free Democrats](/wiki/Free_Democratic_Party_(Germany) "Free Democratic Party (Germany)") in 1991. A special case is the former Federal Minister and Chancellor [Ludwig Erhard](/wiki/Ludwig_Erhard "Ludwig Erhard"), whose affiliation with the [Christian Democratic Union](/wiki/Christian_Democratic_Union_(Germany) "Christian Democratic Union (Germany)") (CDU) has not been conclusively established: although he served as Minister of Economics from 1949 to 1963 and as Federal Chancellor from 1963 to 1966, and was even elected CDU [party chairman](/wiki/Party_chair "Party chair") in 1966, it seems that he never signed a membership form or paid contributions. Researches by *[Der Stern](/wiki/Der_Stern "Der Stern")* magazine have revealed a record at the CDU party archives created only in 1968, with the faked date of entry of early March 1949.[[63]](#cite_note-63)

### Iceland

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Independent_politician&action=edit&section=48 "Edit section: Iceland")]

The [President of Iceland](/wiki/President_of_Iceland "President of Iceland") (currently [Halla Tómasdóttir](/wiki/Halla_T%C3%B3masd%C3%B3ttir "Halla Tómasdóttir")) is independent. By convention, presidents of Iceland usually relinquish any party membership prior to or upon taking office.

### Ireland

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Independent_politician&action=edit&section=49 "Edit section: Ireland")]

Main article: [Independent politician (Ireland)](/wiki/Independent_politician_(Ireland) "Independent politician (Ireland)")

In Ireland, constituency-based proportional representation, the comparative looseness of formal parties, and strong local sentiment have meant that independents have formed a significant part of the parliamentary landscape since the [foundation of the state](/wiki/Irish_Free_State "Irish Free State"): in the early elections to [Dáil Éireann](/wiki/D%C3%A1il_%C3%89ireann_(Irish_Free_State) "Dáil Éireann (Irish Free State)") (parliament), independents accounted for [7% of seats in 1922](/wiki/1922_Irish_general_election "1922 Irish general election"), [8.5% in 1923](/wiki/1923_Irish_general_election "1923 Irish general election"), [10.5% in 1927](/wiki/June_1927_Irish_general_election "June 1927 Irish general election"), and [9% in 1932](/wiki/1932_Irish_general_election "1932 Irish general election"), though with the development of relatively more structured parties their numbers declined thereafter. These were similar proportions to the number of independents elected to other interwar European democracies such as France (see above).

It was not until the 2010s that independents would see a similar electoral success, with record scores for independents surpassing the previous interwar highs.

After the [Irish general election in 2020](/wiki/2020_Irish_general_election "2020 Irish general election"), there were 23 independent [TDs](/wiki/Teachta%C3%AD_D%C3%A1la "Teachtaí Dála") (parliamentary deputies) in the [Dáil](/wiki/D%C3%A1il_%C3%89ireann "Dáil Éireann") (the lower house of the Irish parliament), representing 14% of the total.

There are ten independent senators in the [26th Seanad](/wiki/26th_Seanad "26th Seanad") (the upper house of the Irish parliament), representing 16% of the total. Two of these are elected by the graduates of the [National University of Ireland](/wiki/National_University_of_Ireland "National University of Ireland") and three from [Dublin University](/wiki/Trinity_College_Dublin "Trinity College Dublin"). There is also one independent senator who was nominated by the [Taoiseach](/wiki/Taoiseach "Taoiseach") and four elected by the technical panels.

The [Prime Ministers](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_Italy "Prime Minister of Italy") [Carlo Azeglio Ciampi](/wiki/Carlo_Azeglio_Ciampi "Carlo Azeglio Ciampi") (1993–1994), [Lamberto Dini](/wiki/Lamberto_Dini "Lamberto Dini") (1995–1996), [Giuliano Amato](/wiki/Giuliano_Amato "Giuliano Amato") (2000–2001), [Mario Monti](/wiki/Mario_Monti "Mario Monti") (2011–2013), [Giuseppe Conte](/wiki/Giuseppe_Conte "Giuseppe Conte") (2018–2021) and [Mario Draghi](/wiki/Mario_Draghi "Mario Draghi") (2021–2022) were independent when they were in office.
