The majority of the elected representatives in [Jersey](/wiki/Jersey "Jersey") are independents.

### Kazakhstan

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Independent_politician&action=edit&section=53 "Edit section: Kazakhstan")]

After the dissolution of the [Communist Party of Kazakhstan](/wiki/Communist_Party_of_Kazakhstan_(Soviet_Union) "Communist Party of Kazakhstan (Soviet Union)"), [Nursultan Nazarbayev](/wiki/Nursultan_Nazarbayev "Nursultan Nazarbayev") formally continued to lead the country as an independent politician when being elected in [1991](/wiki/1991_Kazakh_presidential_election "1991 Kazakh presidential election"), before later founding the [Otan](/wiki/Otan_(political_party) "Otan (political party)") (later Nur Otan) party in 1999. Following the [2022 constitutional referendum](/wiki/2022_Kazakh_constitutional_referendum "2022 Kazakh constitutional referendum"), a new clause was added to Article 43(3) of the Constitution stating that, "for the period of exercising his powers, the President of the Republic of Kazakhstan shall not be a member of a political party." This reform re-established the non-partisan status of the presidency, preventing future presidents from holding party membership while in office.[[64]](#cite_note-64) President [Kassym-Jomart Tokayev](/wiki/Kassym-Jomart_Tokayev "Kassym-Jomart Tokayev") subsequently left the [Amanat](/wiki/Amanat_(political_party) "Amanat (political party)") party in 2022 and was [re-elected](/wiki/2022_Kazakh_presidential_election "2022 Kazakh presidential election") later that year as an independent candidate with the support of [People's Coalition](/wiki/People%27s_Coalition_(Kazakhstan) "People's Coalition (Kazakhstan)").[[65]](#cite_note-65) Independent presidential candidates may still receive backing from public associations, including political parties.[[66]](#cite_note-66) By law, presidential candidates must be nominated by a public association, which can include parties, even if they run as independents.

Parliamentary elections in Kazakhstan have historically allowed for both party-affiliated and independent deputies. In the first post-independence [elections of March 1994](/wiki/1994_Kazakh_legislative_election "1994 Kazakh legislative election"), independents won roughly 64 of 177 seats in the [Supreme Council of Kazakhstan](/wiki/Supreme_Council_of_Kazakhstan "Supreme Council of Kazakhstan"). The [1995 elections](/wiki/1995_Kazakh_legislative_election "1995 Kazakh legislative election") for the newly established [Majilis](/wiki/M%C3%A4jilis "Mäjilis") allowed independent, self-nominated candidates to compete alongside party candidates, with independents winning 7 of 67 contested seats. From [2007](/wiki/2007_Kazakh_legislative_election "2007 Kazakh legislative election"), Majilis elections were held entirely through [nationwide](/wiki/Nationwide_constituency "Nationwide constituency") [party-list proportional representation](/wiki/Party-list_proportional_representation "Party-list proportional representation"), preventing independent candidates from winning office. The [mixed electoral system](/wiki/Mixed_electoral_system "Mixed electoral system") was reinstated for the [2023 elections](/wiki/2023_Kazakh_legislative_election "2023 Kazakh legislative election"), allowing independents to run in [single-member districts](/wiki/Single-member_district "Single-member district") while party lists continue to dominate the proportional tier.

The [Senate of Kazakhstan](/wiki/Senate_of_Kazakhstan "Senate of Kazakhstan") remains non-partisan, with senators appointed by the [president](/wiki/President_of_Kazakhstan "President of Kazakhstan") or elected indirectly by local assemblies, rather than through direct party-affiliated elections.

At the local level, *[mäslihats](/wiki/M%C3%A4slihat "Mäslihat")* (regional, city, and district assemblies) also allow independent candidates to run, though recent elections have combined single-member districts and party-list proportional representation. In addition, legal reforms prohibit akims and their deputies from holding positions in party branches or representative offices, restricting formal party roles in local government.[[67]](#cite_note-67)

### Kosovo

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Independent_politician&action=edit&section=54 "Edit section: Kosovo")]

[Atifete Jahjaga](/wiki/Atifete_Jahjaga "Atifete Jahjaga") was elected the first female and Independent [President of Kosovo](/wiki/President_of_Kosovo "President of Kosovo") since the [2008 Kosovo declaration of independence](/wiki/2008_Kosovo_declaration_of_independence "2008 Kosovo declaration of independence"). She was also the first female and independent elected leader in the whole of the [Balkans](/wiki/Balkans "Balkans").

### Liechtenstein

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Independent_politician&action=edit&section=55 "Edit section: Liechtenstein")]

According to the [1862 Constitution of Liechtenstein](/wiki/1862_Constitution_of_Liechtenstein "1862 Constitution of Liechtenstein"), the appointed [Governor of Liechtenstein](/wiki/List_of_heads_of_government_of_Liechtenstein "List of heads of government of Liechtenstein") was required to be politically non-aligned with any party in both Austria and Liechtenstein.[[68]](#cite_note-:12-68) Regardless, however, no political parties existed in Liechtenstein until 1918 and all members of the [Landtag of Liechtenstein](/wiki/Landtag_of_Liechtenstein "Landtag of Liechtenstein") were elected as independents.[[69]](#cite_note-:022-69) Under the currently used [1921 Constitution of Liechtenstein](/wiki/1921_Constitution_of_Liechtenstein "1921 Constitution of Liechtenstein"), independent candidates are allowed to run for both the Landtag and prime minister, though an independent candidate has not been elected to either position since 1921.[[69]](#cite_note-:022-69)[[70]](#cite_note-:02-70)[[71]](#cite_note-officehistory-71)

### The Netherlands

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Independent_politician&action=edit&section=56 "Edit section: The Netherlands")]

[Dick Schoof](/wiki/Dick_Schoof "Dick Schoof") has been the independent [Prime Minister of the Netherlands](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_the_Netherlands "Prime Minister of the Netherlands") since 2024.

### Poland

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Independent_politician&action=edit&section=57 "Edit section: Poland")]

The Polish [Sejm](/wiki/Sejm "Sejm") is elected by party-list ordination, which does not allow lone candidates to run, although since 2001 there has been a possibility to create a non-partisan *Voters' Electoral Committee* (pol. *KWW*, *komitet wyborczy wyborców*); they are by almost any means party lists, but no officially registered party is behind them. They can be unregistered parties, e.g. [Kukiz'15](/wiki/Kukiz%2715 "Kukiz'15"), or non-partisan movements, although the latter never reached the 5% threshold. National minorities candidates also form Voters' Electoral Committees (like [German Minority Electoral Committee](/wiki/German_Minority_Electoral_Committee "German Minority Electoral Committee"), represented in Sejm between 1991 and 2023), but they do not have to reach the nationwide threshold. However, during a Sejm term many members switch parties or become independents.

Tickets such as [Civic Platform](/wiki/Civic_Platform "Civic Platform") during the [2001 election](/wiki/2001_Polish_parliamentary_election "2001 Polish parliamentary election") were formally non-partisan, Civic Platform was widely viewed as a de facto political party, as it is now.

The situation in the [Senate](/wiki/Senate_of_Poland "Senate of Poland") is different, as the voting system allows independents to run as single candidates and some are elected in their own right. In the last parliamentary election ([2023](/wiki/2023_Polish_parliamentary_election "2023 Polish parliamentary election")) four independents won seats in the Senate.

All [presidents](/wiki/President_of_Poland "President of Poland") of Poland have formally been independents. [Lech Wałęsa](/wiki/Lech_Wa%C5%82%C4%99sa "Lech Wałęsa") was not an endorsed candidate of any party, but the chairman of the [Solidarity](/wiki/Solidarity_(Polish_trade_union) "Solidarity (Polish trade union)") and he was elected without full support of the union (with some in Solidarity preferring [Prime Minister](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_Poland "Prime Minister of Poland") [Tadeusz Mazowiecki](/wiki/Tadeusz_Mazowiecki "Tadeusz Mazowiecki")). [Aleksander Kwaśniewski](/wiki/Aleksander_Kwa%C5%9Bniewski "Aleksander Kwaśniewski") was a leader of the [Social Democracy of the Republic of Poland](/wiki/Social_Democracy_of_the_Republic_of_Poland "Social Democracy of the Republic of Poland"), but formally resigned from the party after he was elected, as did [Lech Kaczyński](/wiki/Lech_Kaczy%C5%84ski "Lech Kaczyński"), who was the first leader of [Law and Justice](/wiki/Law_and_Justice_(Poland) "Law and Justice (Poland)") (PiS), [Bronisław Komorowski](/wiki/Bronis%C5%82aw_Komorowski "Bronisław Komorowski") ([PO](/wiki/Civic_Platform "Civic Platform")) and [Andrzej Duda](/wiki/Andrzej_Duda "Andrzej Duda") (PiS). The resignation is required because the Constitution says that the president shall hold no other offices nor discharge any public functions.[[72]](#cite_note-72) The aforementioned presidents often participated in their party's campaigns (e.g. Andrzej Duda in the Law and Justice campaign three months after his resignation from the party). [Karol Nawrocki](/wiki/Karol_Nawrocki "Karol Nawrocki"), despite being endorsed by the Law and Justice party, was never a member of the party and stood as an independent candidate.

### Portugal

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Independent_politician&action=edit&section=58 "Edit section: Portugal")]

[Marcelo Rebelo de Sousa](/wiki/Marcelo_Rebelo_de_Sousa "Marcelo Rebelo de Sousa"), the current [president of Portugal](/wiki/President_of_Portugal "President of Portugal") since 6 March 2016, was elected on 24 January 2016 while being a leading member of the [Social Democratic Party](/wiki/Social_Democratic_Party_(Portugal) "Social Democratic Party (Portugal)"), but suspended his [political affiliation](/wiki/Political_affiliation "Political affiliation") on the day of his [swearing-in](/wiki/Swearing_in "Swearing in").[[73]](#cite_note-73)

### Russia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Independent_politician&action=edit&section=59 "Edit section: Russia")]

All of Russia's [presidents](/wiki/President_of_Russia "President of Russia") have been independents. Former president [Dmitry Medvedev](/wiki/Dmitry_Medvedev "Dmitry Medvedev") declined an offer to join [United Russia](/wiki/United_Russia "United Russia"), saying that he believes the President should be an independent so that he serves the interests of the country rather than his political party.

[Vladimir Putin](/wiki/Vladimir_Putin "Vladimir Putin"), the current president of Russia, was the head of the [United Russia](/wiki/United_Russia "United Russia") party until 26 May 2012, but even then was not its member, thus formally was and still is independent.

### Sweden

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Independent_politician&action=edit&section=60 "Edit section: Sweden")]

The Swedish election system is based on parties nominating candidate MPs for their party ballots, and each party has to receive 4% or more of the national vote (or 12% in one region, which has never happened independently of also reaching the different 4% threshold). This makes running as an independent MP impossible. Once elected, the seat is personal; MPs may resign their party membership, or be stripped of it, while retaining their [Riksdag](/wiki/Riksdag "Riksdag") seats to become independent to become what is commonly referred to as a *politisk vilde* (*political savage*) symbol: (-).

In the [Government](/wiki/Government_of_Sweden "Government of Sweden") (executive cabinet), there is no requirement for ministers to be MPs, or even have a political affiliation (though this has overwhelmingly been the case in modern times). This means that even the [Prime Minister](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_Sweden "Prime Minister of Sweden") could technically be an independent if chosen by the Riksdag.

### The United Kingdom

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Independent_politician&action=edit&section=61 "Edit section: The United Kingdom")]

The [Registration of Political Parties Act 1998](/wiki/Registration_of_Political_Parties_Act_1998 "Registration of Political Parties Act 1998") laid down the first specific rules in the United Kingdom relating to the use of the term 'independent' by election candidates. That Act was repealed with most of its contents covered by Part II of the [Political Parties, Elections and Referendums Act 2000](/wiki/Political_Parties,_Elections_and_Referendums_Act_2000 "Political Parties, Elections and Referendums Act 2000"). Candidates standing for United Kingdom local elections and United Kingdom parliamentary elections, including the devolved parliaments and assemblies, can use the name of a registered political party, or the term 'Independent' (or its Welsh language equivalent *annibynol*) or no ballot paper description at all.[[74]](#cite_note-74)[[75]](#cite_note-75)

Some groups in the United Kingdom who are not affiliated to any national or regional party have registered locality-based political parties. Some English examples are the [Independent Kidderminster Hospital and Health Concern](/wiki/Independent_Kidderminster_Hospital_and_Health_Concern "Independent Kidderminster Hospital and Health Concern"), the [Epsom and Ewell Residents Association](/wiki/Epsom_and_Ewell_Residents_Association "Epsom and Ewell Residents Association"), the [Devizes Guardians](/wiki/Devizes_Guardians "Devizes Guardians"), the [Derwentside Independents](/wiki/Derwentside_Independents "Derwentside Independents"), and the East Yorkshire Independents.[[76]](#cite_note-76)

#### House of Commons

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Independent_politician&action=edit&section=62 "Edit section: House of Commons")]

See also: [List of minor party and independent MPs elected in the United Kingdom](/wiki/List_of_minor_party_and_independent_MPs_elected_in_the_United_Kingdom "List of minor party and independent MPs elected in the United Kingdom")

Before the twentieth century, it was fairly common for independents to be elected to the [House of Commons of the United Kingdom](/wiki/House_of_Commons_of_the_United_Kingdom "House of Commons of the United Kingdom"), but there have been very few since 1945. [S. O. Davies](/wiki/S._O._Davies "S. O. Davies"), a veteran [Labour](/wiki/Labour_Party_(UK) "Labour Party (UK)") MP, held his [Merthyr Tydfil](/wiki/Merthyr_Tydfil "Merthyr Tydfil") seat in the [1970 general election](/wiki/1970_United_Kingdom_general_election "1970 United Kingdom general election"), standing as an independent, after the Labour Party had deselected him.

Journalist [Martin Bell](/wiki/Martin_Bell "Martin Bell") was elected at [Tatton](/wiki/Tatton_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Tatton (UK Parliament constituency)") in the [general election of 1997](/wiki/1997_United_Kingdom_general_election "1997 United Kingdom general election"), having stood on an anti-corruption platform, defeating incumbent [Neil Hamilton](/wiki/Neil_Hamilton_(politician) "Neil Hamilton (politician)"). He was the first independent to be newly elected to the Commons since [1951](/wiki/1951_United_Kingdom_general_election "1951 United Kingdom general election").[[77]](#cite_note-GuardianMartinBell-77) He stood unsuccessfully in a different constituency in 2001.

At the [2001 general election](/wiki/2001_United_Kingdom_general_election "2001 United Kingdom general election"), [Richard Taylor](/wiki/Richard_Taylor_(UK_politician) "Richard Taylor (UK politician)") of the [Independent Kidderminster Hospital and Health Concern](/wiki/Independent_Kidderminster_Hospital_and_Health_Concern "Independent Kidderminster Hospital and Health Concern") party was elected for the constituency of [Wyre Forest](/wiki/Wyre_Forest_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Wyre Forest (UK Parliament constituency)"). Taylor was re-elected for Wyre Forest at the [2005 general election](/wiki/2005_United_Kingdom_general_election "2005 United Kingdom general election"), becoming the only independent in recent times to have been elected for a second term.

Two independent (or local party) members of parliament were elected in the [2005 election](/wiki/2005_United_Kingdom_general_election "2005 United Kingdom general election"), although both were defeated five years later. In the same election, [Peter Law](/wiki/Peter_Law "Peter Law") was elected as an independent at [Blaenau Gwent](/wiki/Blaenau_Gwent_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Blaenau Gwent (UK Parliament constituency)"). Law died on 25 April 2006: the resulting [by-election](/wiki/2006_Blaenau_Gwent_by-elections "2006 Blaenau Gwent by-elections") elected [Dai Davies](/wiki/Dai_Davies_(politician) "Dai Davies (politician)") of the local party [Blaenau Gwent People's Voice](/wiki/Blaenau_Gwent_People%27s_Voice "Blaenau Gwent People's Voice"). The by-election was unusual as it was the first time in over eighty years that an independent had held a seat previously occupied by another independent.

Only one independent was elected to the Commons in the [2010](/wiki/2010_United_Kingdom_general_election "2010 United Kingdom general election"), [2015](/wiki/2015_United_Kingdom_general_election "2015 United Kingdom general election") and [2017 elections](/wiki/2017_United_Kingdom_general_election "2017 United Kingdom general election"): [Sylvia Hermon](/wiki/Sylvia_Hermon "Sylvia Hermon"), the member for [North Down](/wiki/North_Down_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "North Down (UK Parliament constituency)"), a [Unionist](/wiki/Unionism_in_Ireland "Unionism in Ireland") who left the [Ulster Unionist Party](/wiki/Ulster_Unionist_Party "Ulster Unionist Party") because of its links with the [Conservatives](/wiki/Conservative_Party_(UK) "Conservative Party (UK)").

There have also been several instances of politicians being elected to the Commons as representatives of a political party, then resigning the party's whip, or having it withdrawn. Examples in this in the 2010–2015 parliament included [Mike Hancock](/wiki/Mike_Hancock_(British_politician) "Mike Hancock (British politician)") (formerly a [Liberal Democrat](/wiki/Liberal_Democrats_(UK) "Liberal Democrats (UK)")), [Eric Joyce](/wiki/Eric_Joyce "Eric Joyce") (formerly Labour) and [Nadine Dorries](/wiki/Nadine_Dorries "Nadine Dorries"), a Conservative who had the whip withdrawn for part of the parliament and thus sat as an independent during that time.

Independent candidates often stand in British parliamentary elections, often with platforms about specific local issues, but usually without success. An example from the 2001 general election was [Aston Villa](/wiki/Aston_Villa "Aston Villa") supporter Ian Robinson, who stood as an independent in the [Sutton Coldfield constituency](/wiki/Sutton_Coldfield_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Sutton Coldfield (UK Parliament constituency)") in protest at the way chairman [Doug Ellis](/wiki/Doug_Ellis "Doug Ellis") ran the football club. Another example an independent candidate, in the [Salisbury constituency](/wiki/Salisbury_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Salisbury (UK Parliament constituency)"), is [Arthur Uther Pendragon](/wiki/Arthur_Uther_Pendragon "Arthur Uther Pendragon"), a local activist and self-declared reincarnation of [King Arthur](/wiki/King_Arthur "King Arthur").

Other independent candidates are associated with a political party and may be former members of it, but cannot stand under its label. For instance, for several months after being expelled from the Labour Party but before the [Respect Coalition](/wiki/Respect_Party "Respect Party") was founded, [George Galloway](/wiki/George_Galloway "George Galloway") MP described himself as "Independent Labour".

On 23 March 2005, the [Independent Network](/wiki/Independent_Network "Independent Network") was set up to support independent candidates in the forthcoming general election.[[78]](#cite_note-78) The Independent Network still supports Independent candidates in local, regional, national and European elections. It has an organic[*[clarification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*] set of principles which are known as the Bell Principles and are very closely related to [Lord Nolan's Standards of Public Life](/wiki/Committee_on_Standards_in_Public_Life "Committee on Standards in Public Life"). The Independent Network does not impose any [ideology](/wiki/Ideology "Ideology") or political influence on their candidates.

In March 2009, the multi-millionaire [Paul Judge](/wiki/Paul_Judge "Paul Judge") established the [Jury Team](/wiki/Jury_Team "Jury Team"), an umbrella organisation dedicated to increasing the number of independent candidates standing in Britain, in both national and European elections.[[79]](#cite_note-Independant8Mar09-79)

In 2024, a record of six independent candidates was elected to the 59th parliament.[[80]](#cite_note-80)

##### Independent and undescribed candidates

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Independent_politician&action=edit&section=63 "Edit section: Independent and undescribed candidates")]

Part II of the [Political Parties, Elections and Referendums Act 2000](/wiki/Political_Parties,_Elections_and_Referendums_Act_2000 "Political Parties, Elections and Referendums Act 2000") allows individuals who wish to stand as a candidate to all parliaments and assemblies in the UK, including the House of Commons, the right to use one of three ballot paper descriptions. Those descriptions are the name of a registered political party; the word "independent"; or no description at all.[[81]](#cite_note-81)

Unless a candidate stands as "independent" or as a "No Description" candidate leaving the ballot paper description box blank, their candidature must be confirmed by a signed certificate from the relevant officer from a registered political party, as set out in Section 52 of the [Electoral Administration Act 2006](/wiki/Electoral_Administration_Act_2006 "Electoral Administration Act 2006").[[82]](#cite_note-82)

#### House of Lords

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Independent_politician&action=edit&section=64 "Edit section: House of Lords")]

Main articles: [Crossbencher](/wiki/Crossbencher "Crossbencher"), [Non-affiliated members of the House of Lords](/wiki/Non-affiliated_members_of_the_House_of_Lords "Non-affiliated members of the House of Lords"), and [Lords Spiritual](/wiki/Lords_Spiritual "Lords Spiritual")

The [House of Lords](/wiki/House_of_Lords "House of Lords") includes many peers independent from political parties. Some are simply [not affiliated](/wiki/Non-affiliated_members_of_the_House_of_Lords "Non-affiliated members of the House of Lords") with any grouping, whilst another, larger, grouping is given the official designation of [crossbenchers](/wiki/Crossbench#United_Kingdom "Crossbench"). Additionally the [Lords Spiritual](/wiki/Lords_Spiritual "Lords Spiritual") (bishops of the [Church of England](/wiki/Church_of_England "Church of England")) do not have party affiliations.

#### Scottish Parliament, Senedd (Welsh Parliament) and Northern Irish Assembly

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Independent_politician&action=edit&section=65 "Edit section: Scottish Parliament, Senedd (Welsh Parliament) and Northern Irish Assembly")]

In the [2003 Scottish Parliamentary elections](/wiki/2003_Scottish_Parliament_election "2003 Scottish Parliament election"), three [MSPs](/wiki/Member_of_the_Scottish_Parliament "Member of the Scottish Parliament") were elected as Independents: [Dennis Canavan](/wiki/Dennis_Canavan "Dennis Canavan") ([Falkirk West](/wiki/Falkirk_West_(Scottish_Parliament_constituency) "Falkirk West (Scottish Parliament constituency)")), [Jean Turner](/wiki/Jean_Turner "Jean Turner") ([Strathkelvin and Bearsden](/wiki/Strathkelvin_and_Bearsden_(Scottish_Parliament_constituency) "Strathkelvin and Bearsden (Scottish Parliament constituency)")) and [Margo MacDonald](/wiki/Margo_MacDonald "Margo MacDonald") ([Lothians](/wiki/Lothians_(Scottish_Parliament_electoral_region) "Lothians (Scottish Parliament electoral region)")). In 2004 [Campbell Martin](/wiki/Campbell_Martin "Campbell Martin") ([West of Scotland region](/wiki/West_of_Scotland_(Scottish_Parliament_electoral_region) "West of Scotland (Scottish Parliament electoral region)")) left the [Scottish National Party](/wiki/Scottish_National_Party "Scottish National Party") to become an independent and in 2005 [Brian Monteith](/wiki/Brian_Monteith "Brian Monteith") ([Mid Scotland and Fife](/wiki/Mid_Scotland_and_Fife_(Scottish_Parliament_electoral_region) "Mid Scotland and Fife (Scottish Parliament electoral region)")) left the [Conservative Party](/wiki/Conservative_Party_(UK) "Conservative Party (UK)") to become an independent. At the [2007 Scottish Parliamentary elections](/wiki/2007_Scottish_Parliament_election "2007 Scottish Parliament election") Margo MacDonald was again returned as an independent MSP and was elected as an independent for the third time [four years later](/wiki/2011_Scottish_Parliament_election "2011 Scottish Parliament election"). She died in 2014 while still serving as member of the Parliament. As she was elected as an independent regional MSP, there could be no by-election and her seat remained vacant until the [2016 election](/wiki/2016_Scottish_Parliament_election "2016 Scottish Parliament election").[[83]](#cite_note-83)

[Peter Law](/wiki/Peter_Law "Peter Law") was expelled from the Labour Party after standing against an official Labour candidate in Blaenau Gwent at the 2005 UK general election and became an independent in the National Assembly and UK Parliament. In 2006 Peter Law died from a [brain tumour](/wiki/Brain_tumour "Brain tumour") and his wife, [Trish Law](/wiki/Trish_Law "Trish Law"), campaigned and took the seat as an independent candidate at the subsequent by-election and held onto the seat again in the [2007 Welsh Assembly elections](/wiki/2007_National_Assembly_for_Wales_election "2007 National Assembly for Wales election").

In 2016, [Nathan Gill](/wiki/Nathan_Gill "Nathan Gill") as the then leader of UKIP Wales defected from the group to sit as an independent after a falling out with [Neil Hamilton](/wiki/Neil_Hamilton_(politician) "Neil Hamilton (politician)"), who was elected UKIP Assembly group leader. [Dafydd Elis-Thomas](/wiki/Dafydd_Elis-Thomas "Dafydd Elis-Thomas") left the [Plaid Cymru](/wiki/Plaid_Cymru "Plaid Cymru") group later in 2016 after multiple fallings out with Plaid Cymru leader [Leanne Wood](/wiki/Leanne_Wood "Leanne Wood"). Elis-Thomas said his reason for leaving Plaid Cymru was that it not serious about working with the [Welsh Labour](/wiki/Welsh_Labour "Welsh Labour") Government. [Neil McEvoy](/wiki/Neil_McEvoy "Neil McEvoy") was expelled from Plaid Cymru on 16 January 2018 and sat as an independent AM until 2021.[[84]](#cite_note-84) Nathan Gill stood down on 27 December 2017[[85]](#cite_note-85) and was replaced by [Mandy Jones](/wiki/Mandy_Jones_(politician) "Mandy Jones (politician)"). Mandy Jones left the UKIP group on 9 January 2018 over a fallout over her staff.[[86]](#cite_note-86)

#### Local elections

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Independent_politician&action=edit&section=66 "Edit section: Local elections")]

The introduction of [directly elected mayors](/wiki/Directly_elected_mayors_in_England_and_Wales "Directly elected mayors in England and Wales") in several parts of England has witnessed the election of independents to run councils in [Stoke-on-Trent](/wiki/Stoke-on-Trent "Stoke-on-Trent"), [Middlesbrough](/wiki/Middlesbrough_(borough) "Middlesbrough (borough)"), [Bedford](/wiki/Borough_of_Bedford "Borough of Bedford"), [Hartlepool](/wiki/Hartlepool_(borough) "Hartlepool (borough)") and [Mansfield](/wiki/Mansfield_(district) "Mansfield (district)"). The first [Mayor of London](/wiki/Mayor_of_London "Mayor of London"), [Ken Livingstone](/wiki/Ken_Livingstone "Ken Livingstone"), was [first elected](/wiki/2000_London_mayoral_election "2000 London mayoral election") as an independent, having run against the official Labour candidate [Frank Dobson](/wiki/Frank_Dobson "Frank Dobson"). He was subsequently re-admitted to the Labour Party in December 2003 before his first re-election campaign.

Independent candidates frequently stand and are elected to local councils. There is a special Independent group of the [Local Government Association](/wiki/Local_Government_Association "Local Government Association") to cater for them. A number of local authorities have been entirely or almost entirely composed of independent members, such as the [City of London Corporation](/wiki/City_of_London_Corporation "City of London Corporation"), the [Isles of Scilly Council](/wiki/2009_Council_of_the_Isles_of_Scilly_election "2009 Council of the Isles of Scilly election"), [Orkney Islands Council](/wiki/Orkney_Islands_Council "Orkney Islands Council"), [Shetland Islands Council](/wiki/Shetland_Islands_Council "Shetland Islands Council") and [Comhairle nan Eilean Siar](/wiki/Comhairle_nan_Eilean_Siar "Comhairle nan Eilean Siar") (Western Isles Council) in the [Outer Hebrides](/wiki/Outer_Hebrides "Outer Hebrides").

In [2023, Central Bedfordshire](/wiki/2023_Central_Bedfordshire_Council_election "2023 Central Bedfordshire Council election") became the first [unitary authority](/wiki/Unitary_authorities_of_England "Unitary authorities of England") in England to have an independent administration.[[87]](#cite_note-87)

Roughly a quarter of the [police and crime commissioners](/wiki/Police_and_crime_commissioner "Police and crime commissioner") elected in England and Wales in the [2012 election](/wiki/2012_England_and_Wales_police_and_crime_commissioner_elections "2012 England and Wales police and crime commissioner elections") were independents.[[88]](#cite_note-PCCs_uk-88)

### European Parliament

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Independent_politician&action=edit&section=67 "Edit section: European Parliament")]

In the [European Parliament](/wiki/European_Parliament "European Parliament") such politicians are called *[Non-Inscrits](/wiki/Non-Inscrits "Non-Inscrits")*.

## Oceania

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Independent_politician&action=edit&section=68 "Edit section: Oceania")]

### Australia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Independent_politician&action=edit&section=69 "Edit section: Australia")]

Main article: [Independent politicians in Australia](/wiki/Independent_politicians_in_Australia "Independent politicians in Australia")

Independents are a recurrent feature of the federal [Parliament of Australia](/wiki/Parliament_of_Australia "Parliament of Australia"), and they are more commonly elected to state parliaments. There have been up to five independents in every federal parliament since 1990, and independents have won twenty-eight times during national elections in that time. A large proportion of independents are former members of one of Australia's four main parties, the [Australian Labor Party](/wiki/Australian_Labor_Party "Australian Labor Party"), the [Liberal Party of Australia](/wiki/Liberal_Party_of_Australia "Liberal Party of Australia"), the [Australian Greens](/wiki/Australian_Greens "Australian Greens"), or the [National Party of Australia](/wiki/National_Party_of_Australia "National Party of Australia"). In 2013 a political party named the [Australian Independents](/wiki/Australian_Independents "Australian Independents") was registered with the [Australian Electoral Commission](/wiki/Australian_Electoral_Commission "Australian Electoral Commission").[[89]](#cite_note-89)

At the dissolution of parliament before the [2019 federal election](/wiki/2019_Australian_federal_election "2019 Australian federal election"), four independents sat in the [Australian House of Representatives](/wiki/Australian_House_of_Representatives "Australian House of Representatives"): [Andrew Wilkie](/wiki/Andrew_Wilkie "Andrew Wilkie") (Member for [Denison](/wiki/Division_of_Denison "Division of Denison")), [Cathy McGowan](/wiki/Cathy_McGowan_(politician) "Cathy McGowan (politician)") (Member for [Indi](/wiki/Division_of_Indi "Division of Indi")), [Kerryn Phelps](/wiki/Kerryn_Phelps "Kerryn Phelps") (Member for [Wentworth](/wiki/Division_of_Wentworth "Division of Wentworth")), and [Julia Banks](/wiki/Julia_Banks "Julia Banks") (Member for [Chisholm](/wiki/Division_of_Chisholm "Division of Chisholm")). Of these, Wilkie had previously been a Greens candidate, McGowan had been a Liberal staffer, and Banks was elected as a Liberal MP before resigning from the party in November 2018. At the 2019 election, Wilkie was re-elected as the Member for [Clark](/wiki/Division_of_Clark "Division of Clark"), while McGowan retired, and both Phelps and Banks lost their seats. However, two new independents entered parliament: [Zali Steggall](/wiki/Zali_Steggall "Zali Steggall") (Member for [Warringah](/wiki/Division_of_Warringah "Division of Warringah")) and [Helen Haines](/wiki/Helen_Haines "Helen Haines") (Member for [Indi](/wiki/Division_of_Indi "Division of Indi")).

After the [2022 federal election](/wiki/2022_Australian_federal_election "2022 Australian federal election"), a record ten independents were elected to the House of Representatives, including re-elected members Andrew Wilkie ([Clark](/wiki/Division_of_Clark "Division of Clark")), Zali Steggall ([Warringah](/wiki/Division_of_Warringah "Division of Warringah")), and Helen Haines ([Indi](/wiki/Division_of_Indi "Division of Indi")). Seven new independents were elected to the House of Representatives: [Dai Le](/wiki/Dai_Le "Dai Le") ([Fowler](/wiki/Division_of_Fowler "Division of Fowler")), [Zoe Daniel](/wiki/Zoe_Daniel "Zoe Daniel") ([Goldstein](/wiki/Division_of_Goldstein "Division of Goldstein")), [Monique Ryan](/wiki/Monique_Ryan "Monique Ryan") ([Kooyong](/wiki/Division_of_Kooyong "Division of Kooyong")), [Allegra Spender](/wiki/Allegra_Spender "Allegra Spender") ([Wentworth](/wiki/Division_of_Wentworth "Division of Wentworth")), [Kate Chaney](/wiki/Kate_Chaney "Kate Chaney") ([Curtin](/wiki/Division_of_Curtin "Division of Curtin")), [Kylea Tink](/wiki/Kylea_Tink "Kylea Tink") ([North Sydney](/wiki/Division_of_North_Sydney "Division of North Sydney")), and [Sophie Scamps](/wiki/Sophie_Scamps "Sophie Scamps") ([Mackellar](/wiki/Division_of_Mackellar "Division of Mackellar")). Several of the newly elected independents have been branded [Teal independents](/wiki/Teal_independents "Teal independents"), due to their use of the colour teal in campaigning material, similar policy platforms and support from [Climate 200](/wiki/Climate_200 "Climate 200").[[90]](#cite_note-90)

Independent [senators](/wiki/Australian_Senate "Australian Senate") are quite rare. In modern politics, Independent [Brian Harradine](/wiki/Brian_Harradine "Brian Harradine") served from 1975 to 2005 with considerable influence at times. [Nick Xenophon](/wiki/Nick_Xenophon "Nick Xenophon") was the only elected independent senator after his election to the Senate at the [2007 federal election](/wiki/2007_Australian_federal_election "2007 Australian federal election") and was re-elected for another six-year term at the [2013 federal election](/wiki/2013_Australian_federal_election "2013 Australian federal election").[[91]](#cite_note-91) He resigned from the Australian Senate in 2017 to contest a seat in the House of Assembly of South Australia. [DLP](/wiki/Democratic_Labour_Party_(Australia,_1980) "Democratic Labour Party (Australia, 1980)") Senator [John Madigan](/wiki/John_Madigan_(Australian_politician) "John Madigan (Australian politician)") became an independent senator in September 2014,[[92]](#cite_note-92) but lost his seat in the [2016 election](/wiki/2016_Australian_federal_election "2016 Australian federal election"). [PUP](/wiki/Palmer_United_Party "Palmer United Party") Senators [Jacqui Lambie](/wiki/Jacqui_Lambie "Jacqui Lambie") and [Glenn Lazarus](/wiki/Glenn_Lazarus "Glenn Lazarus") became Independent senators in November 2014 and March 2015 respectively.[[93]](#cite_note-93)[[94]](#cite_note-94) Lambie was re-elected in 2019 with the support of the [Jacqui Lambie Network](/wiki/Jacqui_Lambie_Network "Jacqui Lambie Network").[[95]](#cite_note-95) At the [2022 Australian federal election](/wiki/2022_Australian_federal_election "2022 Australian federal election"), independent senator for the ACT [David Pocock](/wiki/David_Pocock "David Pocock") was elected, becoming the first independent senator from a territory.[[96]](#cite_note-96)[[97]](#cite_note-97)

### New Zealand

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Independent_politician&action=edit&section=70 "Edit section: New Zealand")]

Originally, there were no recognised parties in the New Zealand parliament, although loose groupings did exist informally (initially between supporters of central government versus provincial governments, and later between liberals and conservatives). The foundation of formal political parties, starting at the end of the 19th century, considerably diminished the number of unaffiliated politicians, although a smaller number of independent candidates continued to be elected up until the 1940s. Since then, however, there have been relatively few independent politicians in Parliament. No independent candidate has won or held a seat in a general election since [1943](/wiki/1943_New_Zealand_general_election "1943 New Zealand general election"), although two independent candidates have been successful in [by-elections](/wiki/By-election "By-election") (in all cases after having held the seats in question as partisan candidates up until that point). Other politicians have become independents in the course of a parliamentary term, but not been voted into office as such.

The last person to be directly elected to Parliament as an independent in New Zealand was [Winston Peters](/wiki/Winston_Peters "Winston Peters"), who won the [1993 by-election](/wiki/1993_Tauranga_by-election "1993 Tauranga by-election") in [Tauranga](/wiki/Tauranga_(New_Zealand_electorate) "Tauranga (New Zealand electorate)") electorate as an independent after having previously held it as a member of the [National Party](/wiki/New_Zealand_National_Party "New Zealand National Party"). By the time of the [next general election](/wiki/1993_New_Zealand_general_election "1993 New Zealand general election"), he had formed his own party ([New Zealand First](/wiki/New_Zealand_First "New Zealand First")), and thus was no longer standing as an independent. Since that time, the only independents in Parliament have been people who quit or were expelled from their original party but retained their seats without going through a by-election. Some have gone on to found or co-found their own parties, with varying levels of success—examples include [Peter Dunne](/wiki/Peter_Dunne "Peter Dunne"), [Taito Phillip Field](/wiki/Taito_Phillip_Field "Taito Phillip Field"), [Gordon Copeland](/wiki/Gordon_Copeland "Gordon Copeland"), [Tau Henare](/wiki/Tau_Henare "Tau Henare"), and [Alamein Kopu](/wiki/Alamein_Kopu "Alamein Kopu"). Others have joined parties which were then outside Parliament, such as [Frank Grover](/wiki/Frank_Grover "Frank Grover") and [Tuariki Delamere](/wiki/Tuariki_Delamere "Tuariki Delamere").

There were two independent MPs in the [49th New Zealand Parliament](/wiki/49th_New_Zealand_Parliament "49th New Zealand Parliament"): [Chris Carter](/wiki/Chris_Carter_(New_Zealand_politician) "Chris Carter (New Zealand politician)") and [Hone Harawira](/wiki/Hone_Harawira "Hone Harawira"). Carter became an independent after his criticisms of the [Labour Party](/wiki/New_Zealand_Labour_Party "New Zealand Labour Party")'s leadership resulted in his being expelled from the Labour caucus, while Harawira resigned from the [Māori Party](/wiki/M%C4%81ori_Party "Māori Party") and, after a short period as an independent, also resigned as an MP in order to force the [2011 by-election](/wiki/2011_Te_Tai_Tokerau_by-election "2011 Te Tai Tokerau by-election") when he was re-elected as representative of his new political party, [Mana](/wiki/Mana_Party_(New_Zealand) "Mana Party (New Zealand)") and retained the seat in the 2011 General Election. There were also two other parties which had only a single MP: [United Future](/wiki/United_Future "United Future") with [Peter Dunne](/wiki/Peter_Dunne "Peter Dunne") and [ACT](/wiki/ACT_New_Zealand "ACT New Zealand") with [David Seymour](/wiki/David_Seymour "David Seymour"). Neither Dunne nor Seymour was classed as an independent—Dunne's presence in Parliament was due to personal votes in his home electorate, and Seymour's presence was as the sole elected MP of ACT because of a collapse in their support in the [2011 election](/wiki/2011_New_Zealand_general_election "2011 New Zealand general election"). In the [50th New Zealand Parliament](/wiki/50th_New_Zealand_Parliament "50th New Zealand Parliament") there was one independent MP: [Brendan Horan](/wiki/Brendan_Horan "Brendan Horan"), a former New Zealand First MP who was expelled from his party because of allegations of misappropriation of family assets.

Peter Dunne effectively became an Independent MP for a short period after his United Future political party was deregistered on 25 June 2013 by the Electoral Commission, as the party no longer had the required minimum of 500 members.[[98]](#cite_note-98) The party was subsequently re-registered two months later.[[99]](#cite_note-99)[[100]](#cite_note-100)

### Niue

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Independent_politician&action=edit&section=71 "Edit section: Niue")]

In [Niue](/wiki/Niue "Niue"), there have been no political parties since 2003, when the [Niue People's Party](/wiki/Niue_People%27s_Party "Niue People's Party") disbanded, and all politicians are *de facto* independents. The government depends on an informal coalition.[[101]](#cite_note-101)
